title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true.
rationale: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit privileged containers. If you need to run privileged containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true, spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
audit: Run the following command: get pods -A -o=jsonpath=$'{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.name}: {@..securityContext}\n{end}' It will produce an inventory of all the privileged use on the cluster, if any (please, refer to a sample below). Further grepping can be done to automate each specific violation detection. calico-kube-controllers-57b57c56f-jtmk4: {} << No Elevated Privileges calico-node- c4xv4: {} {"privileged":true} {"privileged":true} {"privileged":true} {"privileged":true} << Violates 5.2.2 dashboard-metrics-scraper-7bc864c59-2m2xw: {"seccompProfile":{"type":"RuntimeDefault"}} {"allowPrivilegeEscalation":false,"readOnlyRootFilesystem":true,"runAsGroup":2001,"ru nAsUser":1001}
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of privileged containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of privileged containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true, spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be audited using an automated approach through Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The audit steps provided in the compliance control data can be effectively executed using automation to check for privileged containers across all namespaces, making this approach feasible and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods -A -o json - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged`, `spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged`, `spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged` - Pass Criteria: No containers (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) in any Pod have `securityContext.privileged: true`. - Fail Criteria: Any container, initContainer, or ephemeralContainer in any Pod has `securityContext.privileged: true`.

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host process ID namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's PID namespace can inspect processes running outside the container. If the container also has access to ptrace capabilities this can be used to escalate privileges outside of the container. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host PID namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostPID, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.hostPID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostPID containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostPID containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPID containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostPID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach, confirming that the audit can be effectively performed using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. The audit steps outlined in the compliance control data can be executed programmatically, making automation feasible and efficient for ensuring compliance with the hostPID restrictions.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA) and PodSecurityPolicies (PSP) - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: - For PSA: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` label values (should be at least `baseline` or `restricted`) - For PSP: Ensure that `spec.hostPID` is set to `false` or not present, and that no policy allows `hostPID: true` except for authorized service accounts. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace with user workloads must have Pod Security Admission enforced at `baseline` or `restricted`, or have PodSecurityPolicies that do not allow `hostPID: true` except for tightly controlled exceptions. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace that allows pods with `spec.hostPID: true` without explicit, minimal exceptions or lacks the necessary PSA or PSP configurations.

If you need to run containers which require hostIPC, this should be definited in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.hostIPC: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostIPC containers

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host network namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostNetwork flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's network namespace could access the local loopback device, and could access network traffic to and from other pods. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host network namespace. If you need to run containers which require access to the host's network namesapces, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.hostNetwork: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostNetwork containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostNetwork containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostNetwork containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostNetwork: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with allowPrivilegeEscalation
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true. Allowing this right can lead to a process running a container getting more rights than it started with. It's important to note that these rights are still constrained by the overall container sandbox, and this setting does not relate to the use of privileged containers.
rationale: A container running with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true may have processes that can gain more privileges than their parent. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to allow privilege escalation. The option exists (and is defaulted to true) to permit setuid binaries to run. If you have need to run containers which use setuid binaries or require privilege escalation, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which allow privilege escalation.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of conatiners with .spec.allowPrivilegeEscalationset to true. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on contained process ability to escalate privileges, within the context of the container. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as both AI opinions support an automated approach. The first opinion provides a comprehensive method for checking compliance through the use of Pod Security Admission labels and PodSecurityPolicies, while the second opinion reinforces the need for direct inspection of running pods, which can also be automated. Given that all necessary checks can be performed programmatically, automation is the most efficient and reliable approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces, PodSecurityPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get ns --show-labels`, `kubectl get psp -o yaml`, `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation}'` - Properties to Check: - Namespace labels for Pod Security Admission (PSA) set to at least `baseline` or `restricted`. - PodSecurityPolicies that enforce `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`. - Running pods' `securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation` field. - Pass Criteria: - Every namespace with user workloads must have a PSA label set to at least `baseline` or `restricted`, or an equivalent PodSecurityPolicy that enforces `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`. - No namespace should allow unrestricted privilege escalation via policy. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacking a PSA label or with a label below `baseline`, or with a PodSecurityPolicy that allows `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`.

title: Minimize the admission of root containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run as the root user.
rationale: Containers may run as any Linux user. Containers which run as the root user, whilst constrained by Container Runtime security features still have a escalated likelihood of container breakout. Ideally, all containers should run as a defined non-UID 0 user. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit root containers. If you need to run root containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy restricts the use of root containers by setting MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0.
remediation: Create a policy for each namespace in the cluster, ensuring that either MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0, is set. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of root containers and if a User is not specified in the image, the container will run as root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
justification: The audit control for minimizing the admission of root containers can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of an automated approach. The proposed automation details leverage existing Kubernetes capabilities to check for compliance with the specified policies, ensuring that every namespace has the necessary restrictions in place.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces, Pod Security Admission (PSA), PodSecurityPolicies (PSP), OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get namespaces` - `kubectl get namespace <ns> -o json` - `kubectl get psp -o yaml` (if applicable) - Properties to Check: - For PSA: Check `metadata.labels` for `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `audit`, or `warn` set to `restricted`. - For PSP: Ensure `spec.runAsUser.rule` is set to `MustRunAsNonRoot` or `MustRunAs` with a UID range excluding 0. - For OPA Gatekeeper/Kyverno: Verify policies that enforce `runAsNonRoot: true`. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace must have at least one enforced policy preventing containers from running as root (UID 0). - Fail Criteria: Any namespace lacking such a policy, or with policies that allow containers to run as root, is non-compliant.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability
description: Do not generally permit containers with the potentially dangerous NET_RAW capability.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. By default this can include potentially dangerous capabilities. With Docker as the container runtime the NET_RAW capability is enabled which may be misused by malicious containers. Ideally, all containers should drop this capability. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers with the NET_RAW capability. If you need to run containers with this capability, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy disallows the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with the NET_RAW capability. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach, highlighting the feasibility of using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands to check for policies related to the NET_RAW capability. The audit steps outlined in the compliance control can be effectively executed using `kubectl` commands to enumerate namespaces and verify the presence of appropriate admission control policies. This aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes, making automation a suitable choice.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces, Pod Security Admission, PodSecurityPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: - Namespace labels for Pod Security Admission (e.g., `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted`) - PodSecurityPolicies for `allowedCapabilities` and `requiredDropCapabilities` - Admission controller policies (e.g., OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) for restrictions on `NET_RAW` - Pass Criteria: At least one policy in each namespace with user workloads that denies the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability. - Fail Criteria: Absence of any policy in a namespace that restricts the NET_RAW capability.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with added capabilities
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities assigned beyond the default set.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities outside this set can be added to containers which could expose them to risks of container breakout attacks. There should be at least one policy defined which prevents containers with capabilities beyond the default set from launching. If you need to run containers with additional capabilities, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities outwith the default set will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that policies are present which prevent allowedCapabilities to be set to anything other than an empty array.
remediation: Ensure that allowedCapabilities is not present in policies for the cluster unless it is set to an empty array. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on adding capabilities to containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities outwith the default set will not be permitted.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. The automation details provided are comprehensive and leverage existing Kubernetes features such as Pod Security Policies (PSPs) and Pod Security Admission (PSA), which allow for the validation of `allowedCapabilities`. This aligns with the rationale of minimizing risks associated with additional capabilities in containers.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Policies (PSPs), Pod Security Admission (PSA) - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: `allowedCapabilities` field in PSPs or equivalent constraints in PSA - Pass Criteria: `allowedCapabilities` must be either not present or set to an empty array (`[]`) - Fail Criteria: Any policy with `allowedCapabilities` set to a non-empty array

title: Minimize the admission of containers with capabilities assigned
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities are parts of the rights generally granted on a Linux system to the root user. In many cases applications running in containers do not require any capabilities to operate, so from the perspective of the principal of least privilege use of capabilities should be minimized. Impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy requires that capabilities are dropped by all containers.
remediation: Review the use of capabilities in applications running on your cluster. Where a namespace contains applications which do not require any Linux capabilities to operate consider adding a policy which forbids the admission of containers which do not drop all capabilities. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with additional capabilities References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
justification: The audit control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands to check for Pod Security Admission (PSA) labels and ensure compliance with the requirement to drop capabilities. While the second AI opinion raised concerns about the reliance on deprecated PodSecurityPolicies, the focus on PSA is valid and aligns with current Kubernetes security practices. Therefore, an automated approach is appropriate and feasible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces, Pod Security Admission - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get ns --show-labels`, `kubectl get psp -o yaml` (if applicable) - Properties to Check: - For PSA: Check for `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted` or higher. - For PSP (if still in use): Ensure `spec.requiredDropCapabilities` is set to `["ALL"]` or `spec.allowedCapabilities` is empty. - Pass Criteria: - Every namespace must have either: - PSA enforce label set to `restricted` (or stricter), OR - An active PodSecurityPolicy that requires all capabilities to be dropped and does not allow additional capabilities. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace that does not meet the above criteria indicates non-compliance.

title: Minimize the admission of Windows HostProcess Containers

As per the Kubernetes documentation, this provides "privileged access" to the Windows node. Where Windows containers are used inside a Kubernetes cluster, there should be at least one admission control policy which does not permit hostProcess Windows containers. If you need to run Windows containers which require hostProcess, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostProcess containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostProcess containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostProcess containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/create-hostprocess-pod/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation. The first AI opinion provides a comprehensive automation plan that leverages existing Kubernetes capabilities, while the second opinion validates the approach and emphasizes the importance of focusing on Pod Security Admission (PSA) rather than deprecated PodSecurityPolicies. automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces`, `kubectl get psp`, `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o yaml` - Properties to Check: - For PSA: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn` - For Pods: `spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess` - Pass Criteria: - Every namespace with user workloads has a Pod Security Admission policy that restricts the admission of pods with `windowsOptions.hostProcess: true`, OR - No PodSecurityPolicy or admission controller allows `hostProcess: true` except for explicitly controlled exceptions. - No pods are running with `spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true`. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace with user workloads lacks a restrictive Pod Security Admission policy. - Any PodSecurityPolicy allows `hostProcess: true` without controlled exceptions. - Any pod is found running with `spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true`.

title: Minimize the admission of HostPath volumes
description: Do not generally admit containers which make use of hostPath volumes.

The use of hostPath volumes may allow containers access to privileged areas of the node filesystem. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to mount hostPath volumes. If you need to run containers which require hostPath volumes, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a spefific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers with hostPath volumes.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPath volumes. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPath volumes. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a spefific policy.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. The proposed automation details effectively leverage `kubectl` commands to check for Pod Security Admission labels and PodSecurityPolicies, which are all accessible through the Kubernetes API. automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission, PodSecurityPolicy - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get ns --show-labels`, `kubectl get psp` - Properties to Check: - For namespaces: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` should be set to `restricted`. - For PodSecurityPolicies: `volumes` field should not allow `hostPath`, or if allowed, should be tightly scoped in `allowedHostPaths`. - Pass Criteria: - Every namespace with user workloads must have Pod Security Admission enforced at the `restricted` level, or an equivalent policy that denies `hostPath` volumes except for explicitly allowed paths. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace with user workloads that does not have the `restricted` level enforced or has PodSecurityPolicies allowing `hostPath` volumes without strict controls.

title: Minimize the admission of containers which use HostPorts
description: Do not generally permit containers which require the use of HostPorts.
rationale: Host ports connect containers directly to the host's network. This can bypass controls such as network policy. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers which require the use of HostPorts. If you need to run containers which require HostPorts, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which have hostPort sections.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPort sections. impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.

title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None
audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
remediation: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
impact: None

justification: The determination of whether the CNI plugin supports Network Policies requires consulting the specific documentation of the CNI plugin, which is not accessible through Kubernetes APIs or CLI commands. Consult the official documentation for the identified CNI plugin: - Access the documentation (e.g., via the CNI plugin's GitHub repository or official website) and verify that it explicitly states support for both Ingress and Egress Network Policies. 3. title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace. Impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them. Default Value: By default, network policies are not created. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/networkpolicies/ 2. https://octetz.com/posts/k8s-network-policy-apis 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/declare-network-policy/
impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be fully automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The task of checking for the presence of NetworkPolicy objects in each namespace does not require manual intervention, making it suitable for automation. The technical details provided in the opinions confirm the feasibility of this approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: NetworkPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace>` - Properties to Check: Ensure that each namespace has at least one NetworkPolicy object defined. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace in the cluster contains at least one NetworkPolicy object. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace exists with zero NetworkPolicy objects.

title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
rationale: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs. Impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
audit: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
remediation: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables. Default Value: By default, secrets are not defined References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
justification: The task of identifying the use of secrets as environment variables can be efficiently automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools. Both AI opinions agree that an automated approach is suitable, and the provided commands demonstrate the feasibility of querying Kubernetes resources to check for compliance. The automation will streamline the process and reduce the risk of human error.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, ReplicaSets, Jobs, CronJobs - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[*].env[*].valueFrom.secretKeyRef` and `spec.initContainers[*].env[*].valueFrom.secretKeyRef` - Pass Criteria: No containers in any PodSpec-based resource use `env.valueFrom.secretKeyRef` - Fail Criteria: One or more containers in any PodSpec-based resource use `env.valueFrom.secretKeyRef` This program will ensure that all resources are checked for compliance with the control to prefer using secrets as files over environment variables.

title: Consider external secret storage
description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
rationale: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments. Impact: None
audit: Review your secrets management implementation.
remediation: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution. Default Value: By default, no external secret management is configured.
impact: None
justification: The control requires a review of architectural documentation and deployment manifests to determine the integration of external secret management solutions, which cannot be reliably assessed through Kubernetes APIs or automated tools. Both AI opinions agree that human judgment is necessary for this evaluation due to the lack of direct API support for identifying external secret management usage.
manual_summary: 1. Review your cluster's architectural documentation and deployment manifests to identify if an external secrets management solution (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) is integrated with Kubernetes. 2. Inspect the cluster for the presence of third-party secrets controllers or operators by executing the command: ```bash kubectl get deployments --all-namespaces ``` Look for known external secrets management solutions such as External Secrets Operator, Vault Agent Injector, or CSI Secret Drivers in the output. 3. Examine application manifests for how secrets are referenced. Check for usage of CSI drivers, external secret Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs), or annotations indicating external secret injection. This can be done by reviewing the deployment configurations: ```bash kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o yaml | grep -i "external-secrets" ```

title: Configure Image Provenance using ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller
description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
rationale: Kubernetes supports plugging in provenance rules to accept or reject the images in your deployments. You could configure such rules to ensure that only approved images are deployed in the cluster. Impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that image provenance is configured as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup image provenance. Default Value: By default, image provenance is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#imagepolicywebhook 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design- proposals/image-provenance.md 3. https://hub.docker.com/r/dnurmi/anchore-toolbox/ 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/22888
impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be automated through the Kubernetes API and the `kubectl` command-line tool. The steps outlined for checking the `ImagePolicyWebhook` admission controller's configuration can be executed without manual intervention, making it suitable for an automated approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: API Server - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: Command-line arguments for `--enable-admission-plugins` or `--admission-control` containing `ImagePolicyWebhook` - Pass Criteria: The API server must have `ImagePolicyWebhook` listed in its enabled admission plugins. - Fail Criteria: Absence of `ImagePolicyWebhook` in the enabled admission plugins list.

title: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
rationale: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default. You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users. Impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
audit: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them. Default Value: By default, Kubernetes starts with 4 initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with- objects/namespaces/#viewing-namespaces 2. http://blog.kubernetes.io/2016/08/security-best-practices-kubernetes- deployment.html 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-node/589- efficient-node-heartbeats
impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands to enumerate and verify namespaces, which aligns with the capabilities outlined in both AI opinions. The first opinion provides a comprehensive automated approach, while the second opinion supports automation but suggests additional manual checks for RBAC, which can be integrated into the automated process. Given the technical feasibility of using automation for the primary checks, I conclude that an automated approach is appropriate.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: Existence of non-default namespaces and distribution of resources across them - Pass Criteria: At least one non-default namespace exists, and resources (Deployments, Pods, Services, etc.) are present in non-default namespaces. - Fail Criteria: Only the four default namespaces exist, or all user workloads are deployed in the `default` namespace.

title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container. Impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault Default Value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ 2. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/
impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the task can be automated using Kubernetes APIs or CLI tools, and the first opinion provides a comprehensive plan for automation. The audit steps outlined in the compliance control data can be effectively executed through a program that checks pod and container security contexts for the seccomp profile. The capabilities of Kubernetes APIs support this automation, making it feasible to implement a solution that checks compliance without manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type for both pod-level and container-level settings - Pass Criteria: Every Pod and every container must have seccompProfile.type set to RuntimeDefault. - Fail Criteria: If the field is missing or set to Unconfined or another value, it indicates non-compliance.

title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context. Impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers. Default Value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/security-context/ 2. https://learn.cisecurity.org/benchmarks
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach, confirming that the control can be effectively checked using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. The automation details provided are technically sound and align with best practices for scalability and efficiency in a Kubernetes environment. The ability to enumerate Pods and check for security contexts programmatically supports the decision for automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: .spec.securityContext at the Pod level and .securityContext for each container within the Pod - Pass Criteria: Each Pod and each container within the Pod must have a securityContext defined (i.e., the field exists and is not null). - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container lacking a securityContext field is non-compliant.

title: The default namespace should not be used
description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
rationale: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources. Impact: None
audit: Run this command to list objects in default namespace kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default The only entries there should be system managed resources such as the kubernetes service
remediation: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace. Default Value: Unless a namespace is specific on object creation, the default namespace will be used
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated classification, and the use of `kubectl` commands effectively allows for the enumeration and checking of resources in the `default` namespace. The automation details provided are technically sound and leverage Kubernetes capabilities to perform the compliance check efficiently.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaced resources (e.g., Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: Existence of user-created resources in the `default` namespace - Pass Criteria: Only system-managed resources (such as the default `kubernetes` service) exist in the `default` namespace; no user/application workloads or configuration objects are present. - Fail Criteria: Any user-created resource (other than system-managed) exists in the `default` namespace.

title: Ensure that the API server pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: The control requires verification of file permissions on the host operating system, which cannot be accessed or modified through Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. Both AI opinions agree that direct access to the control plane node is necessary, confirming that a manual approach is the only viable option for this compliance check.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Kubernetes control plane node where the kube-apiserver is running. 2. Identify the location of the kube-apiserver pod specification file (commonly located at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`). 3. Run the following command to check the file permissions: ``` stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 4. Verify that the output is 600 or more restrictive (e.g., 600, 400, 440, 444). 5. If the permissions are more permissive than 600 (e.g., 640, 644, 660, 666, etc.), remediate by running: ``` chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 6. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and ensuring the permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive.

title: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root.
rationale: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file ownership is set to root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual approach due to the necessity of OS-level access to verify file ownership, which is not possible through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps explicitly require running commands on the control plane node, confirming that automation is not feasible for this specific check.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Kubernetes control plane node where the kube-apiserver is running. 2. Identify the location of the kube-apiserver manifest file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml). 3. Run the following command to check the file ownership: ``` stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 4. Verify that the output is: ``` root:root ``` 5. If the ownership is not root:root, remediate by running: ``` chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 6. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and ensuring the ownership is set to root:root.

title: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The controller manager pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the Controller Manager on the master node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-controller-manager.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual approach due to the necessity of checking file permissions directly on the host filesystem, which is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Kubernetes control plane node where the kube-controller-manager runs. 2. Locate the kube-controller-manager pod specification file (commonly at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml`). 3. Run the following command to check the file permissions: ``` stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml ``` 4. Verify that the output is `600` or a more restrictive value (e.g., `400`). The file should not be group- or world-readable/writable. 5. If the permissions are more permissive than `600` (e.g., `640`, `644`, `660`, `666`, etc.), the control is NOT compliant. 6. If the permissions are `600` or more restrictive, the control is compliant.

title: Ensure that the --token-auth-file parameter is not set
description: Do not use token based authentication.
rationale: The token-based authentication utilizes static tokens to authenticate requests to the apiserver. The tokens are stored in clear-text in a file on the apiserver, and cannot be revoked or rotated without restarting the apiserver. Hence, do not use static token- based authentication. Impact: You will have to configure and use alternate authentication mechanisms such as certificates. Static token based authentication could not be used.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --token-auth-file argument does not exist. Alternative Audit Method kubectl get pod -nkube-system -lcomponent=kube-apiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.spec.containers[*].command} {"\n"}{end}' | grep '\--token-auth- file' | grep -i false If the exit code is '1', then the control isn't present / failed
remediation: Follow the documentation and configure alternate mechanisms for authentication. Then, edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- apiserver.yaml on the master node and remove the --token-auth- file=<filename> parameter. Default Value: By default, --token-auth-file argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/authentication/#static-token-file 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: You will have to configure and use alternate authentication mechanisms such as certificates. Static token based authentication could not be used.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated using kubectl commands or Kubernetes API calls. The kube-apiserver Pod specifications can be queried without manual intervention, making it suitable for automation. The provided command effectively checks for the presence of the `--token-auth-file` flag, which aligns with the compliance control requirements.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath="{range .items[*]}{.spec.containers[*].command} {\"\\n\"}{end}" - Properties to Check: Presence of `--token-auth-file` in the command arguments of kube-apiserver containers - Pass Criteria: None of the kube-apiserver containers have the `--token-auth-file` flag set. - Fail Criteria: Any kube-apiserver container has the `--token-auth-file` flag set (regardless of value).

title: Ensure that the DenyServiceExternalIPs is set
description: This admission controller rejects all net-new usage of the Service field externalIPs.
rationale: Most users do not need the ability to set the externalIPs field for a Service at all, and cluster admins should consider disabling this functionality by enabling the DenyServiceExternalIPs admission controller. Clusters that do need to allow this functionality should consider using some custom policy to manage its usage. Impact: When enabled, users of the cluster may not create new Services which use externalIPs and may not add new values to externalIPs on existing Service objects.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the `DenyServiceExternalIPs' argument exist as a string value in --enable- admission-plugins.
remediation: Edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- apiserver.yaml on the master node and append the Kubernetes API server flag -- enable-admission-plugins with the DenyServiceExternalIPs plugin. Note, the Kubernetes API server flag --enable-admission-plugins takes a comma-delimited list of admission control plugins to be enabled, even if they are in the list of plugins enabled by default. kube-apiserver --enable-admission-plugins=DenyServiceExternalIPs Default Value: By default, --enable-admission-plugins=DenyServiceExternalIP argument is not set, and the use of externalIPs is authorized. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/admission-controllers/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: When enabled, users of the cluster may not create new Services which use externalIPs and may not add new values to externalIPs on existing Service objects.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be effectively audited using automated methods by querying the kube-apiserver configuration. automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: kube-apiserver - API/CLI Method: `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml` - Properties to Check: Presence of the `--enable-admission-plugins` flag and inclusion of `DenyServiceExternalIPs` in its value. - Pass Criteria: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag must be present and include `DenyServiceExternalIPs` in its comma-separated list. - Fail Criteria: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag is missing, or `DenyServiceExternalIPs` is not included in its value.

title: Ensure that the --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet- client-key arguments are set as appropriate
description: Enable certificate based kubelet authentication.
rationale: The apiserver, by default, does not authenticate itself to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoints. The requests from the apiserver are treated anonymously. You should set up certificate- based kubelet authentication to ensure that the apiserver authenticates itself to kubelets when submitting requests. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet-client-key arguments exist and they are set as appropriate. Alternative Audit kubectl get pod -nkube-system -lcomponent=kube-apiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[]}{.spec.containers[].command} {"\n"}{end}' | grep '--kubelet-client- certificate' | grep -i false If the exit code is '1', then the control isn't present / failed
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and set up the TLS connection between the apiserver and kubelets. Then, edit API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the kubelet client certificate and key parameters as below. --kubelet-client-certificate=<path/to/client-certificate-file> --kubelet-client-key=<path/to/client-key-file> Default Value: By default, certificate-based kubelet authentication is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/ 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/master-node- communication/#apiserver---kubelet
impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.

This aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes to perform such inspections programmatically.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pod - Properties to Check: .spec.containers[].command for `--kubelet-client-certificate` and `--kubelet-client-key` - Pass Criteria: Both `--kubelet-client-certificate` and `--kubelet-client-key` arguments are present and set to non-empty, valid file paths. - Fail Criteria: Either argument is missing, empty, or set to an invalid value (e.g., "false" or not set at all).

title: Ensure that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument is set as appropriate
description: Verify kubelet's certificate before establishing connection.
rationale: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument exists and is set as appropriate. Alternative Audit kubectl get pod -nkube-system -lcomponent=kube-apiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[]}{.spec.containers[].command} {"\n"}{end}' | grep '--kubelet- certificate-Authority' | grep -i false If the exit code is '1', then the control isn't present / failed
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup the TLS connection between the apiserver and kubelets. Then, edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the --kubelet-certificate-authority parameter to the path to the cert file for the certificate authority. --kubelet-certificate-authority=<ca-string> Default Value: By default, --kubelet-certificate-authority argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/ 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/master-node- communication/#apiserver---kubelet
impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be performed using `kubectl` commands without requiring manual intervention on the host level. The task involves querying the kube-apiserver Pod specifications, which is feasible through Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl`. The automated approach is efficient and aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes for compliance checks.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pod -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[]}{.spec.containers[].command}{"\n"}{end}' - Properties to Check: Presence and validity of the `--kubelet-certificate-authority` argument - Pass Criteria: The `--kubelet-certificate-authority` argument is present and points to a valid certificate authority file (not empty, not "false"). - Fail Criteria: The argument is missing, empty, or explicitly set to "false".

title: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow

title: Ensure that the admission control plugin EventRateLimit is set
description: Limit the rate at which the API server accepts requests.
rationale: Using EventRateLimit admission control enforces a limit on the number of events that the API Server will accept in a given time slice. A misbehaving workload could overwhelm and DoS the API Server, making it unavailable. This particularly applies to a multi-tenant cluster, where there might be a small percentage of misbehaving tenants which could have a significant impact on the performance of the cluster overall. Hence, it is recommended to limit the rate of events that the API server will accept. Impact: You need to carefully tune in limits as per your environment.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --enable-admission-plugins argument is set to a value that includes EventRateLimit.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and set the desired limits in a configuration file. Then, edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- apiserver.yaml and set the below parameters. --enable-admission-plugins=...,EventRateLimit,... --admission-control-config-file=<path/to/configuration/file> Default Value: By default, EventRateLimit is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/admission- controllers/#eventratelimit 3. https://github.com/staebler/community/blob/9873b632f4d99b5d99c38c9b15fe2f8 b93d0a746/contributors/design- proposals/admission_control_event_rate_limit.md
impact: You need to carefully tune in limits as per your environment.
justification: Both AI opinions support the automated approach, indicating that the configuration of the EventRateLimit admission control plugin can be verified using `kubectl` commands. The first opinion provides a clear method for checking the necessary parameters, while the second opinion acknowledges the validity of the automated approach, despite noting potential access limitations. Given that the required checks can be performed through Kubernetes CLI methods, I conclude that an automated solution is feasible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: API Server - API/CLI Method: `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o json` - Properties to Check: - `--enable-admission-plugins` must include `EventRateLimit` - `--admission-control-config-file` must be set to a valid configuration file - Pass Criteria: - The API server manifest or running pod spec includes `--enable-admission-plugins` with `EventRateLimit` listed, and `--admission-control-config-file` is present. - Fail Criteria: - Either `EventRateLimit` is missing from `--enable-admission-plugins`, or `--admission-control-config-file` is not set.

rationale: Setting admission control plugin AlwaysAdmit allows all requests and do not filter any requests. The AlwaysAdmit admission controller was deprecated in Kubernetes v1.13. Its behavior was equivalent to turning off all admission controllers. Impact: Only requests explicitly allowed by the admissions control plugins would be served.

References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/admission- controllers/#alwaysadmit
impact: Only requests explicitly allowed by the admissions control plugins would be served.

title: Ensure that the admission control plugin AlwaysPullImages is set
description: Always pull images.
rationale: Setting admission control policy to AlwaysPullImages forces every new pod to pull the required images every time. In a multi-tenant cluster users can be assured that their private images can only be used by those who have the credentials to pull them. Without this admission control policy, once an image has been pulled to a node, any pod from any user can use it simply by knowing the image’s name, without any authorization check against the image ownership. When this plug-in is enabled, images are always pulled prior to starting containers, which means valid credentials are required. Impact: Credentials would be required to pull the private images every time. Also, in trusted environments, this might increases load on network, registry, and decreases speed. This setting could impact offline or isolated clusters, which have images preloaded and do not have access to a registry to pull in-use images. This setting is not appropriate for clusters which use this configuration.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --enable-admission-plugins argument is set to a value that includes AlwaysPullImages.

Default Value: By default, AlwaysPullImages is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/admission- controllers/#alwayspullimages
impact: Credentials would be required to pull the private images every time. Also, in trusted environments, this might increases load on network, registry, and decreases speed. This setting could impact offline or isolated clusters, which have images preloaded and do not have access to a registry to pull in-use images. This setting is not appropriate for clusters which use this configuration.

The process of checking the `--enable-admission-plugins` flag is straightforward and can be executed programmatically, making automation a viable and efficient solution.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: kube-apiserver - API/CLI Method: `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o json` - Properties to Check: `command` or `args` field for the `--enable-admission-plugins` flag - Pass Criteria: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag is present and includes `AlwaysPullImages` in its value. - Fail Criteria: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag is missing, or `AlwaysPullImages` is not included in its value.

impact: None

title: Ensure that the API Server only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
description: Ensure that the API server is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
rationale: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS cipher suites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided. Impact: API server clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the API server.

title: Ensure that the --service-account-extend-token-expiration parameter is set to false
description: By default Kubernetes extends service account token lifetimes to one year to aid in transition from the legacy token settings.
rationale: This default setting is not ideal for security as it ignores other settings related to maximum token lifetime and means that a lost or stolen credential could be valid for an extended period of time. Impact: Disabling this setting means that the service account token expiry set in the cluster will be enforced, and service account tokens will expire at the end of that time frame.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --service-account-extend-token-expiration argument is set to false.
remediation: Edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the --service-account-extend-token-expiration parameter to false. --service-account-extend-token-expiration=false Default Value: By default, this parameter is set to true References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- apiserver/
impact: Disabling this setting means that the service account token expiry set in the cluster will be enforced, and service account tokens will expire at the end of that time frame.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively checked using Kubernetes CLI commands to inspect the kube-apiserver configuration, which aligns with both AI opinions that support an automated approach. The use of `kubectl` to query the running configuration is feasible and does not require manual file access, making automation a suitable choice.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath="{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}"` - Properties to Check: The presence and value of the `--service-account-extend-token-expiration` argument. - Pass Criteria: The flag `--service-account-extend-token-expiration` is explicitly set to `false`. - Fail Criteria: The flag is absent (defaults to `true`) or set to `true`.

title: Ensure that the --terminated-pod-gc-threshold argument is set as appropriate
description: Activate garbage collector on pod termination, as appropriate.
rationale: Garbage collection is important to ensure sufficient resource availability and avoiding degraded performance and availability. In the worst case, the system might crash or just be unusable for a long period of time. The current setting for garbage collection is 12,500 terminated pods which might be too high for your system to sustain. Based on your system resources and tests, choose an appropriate threshold value to activate garbage collection. Impact: None
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-controller-manager Verify that the --terminated-pod-gc-threshold argument is set as appropriate.
remediation: Edit the Controller Manager pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- controller-manager.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the --terminated- pod-gc-threshold to an appropriate threshold, for example: --terminated-pod-gc-threshold=10 Default Value: By default, --terminated-pod-gc-threshold is set to 12500. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-controller-manager/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/28484
impact: None
justification: The task of verifying the `--terminated-pod-gc-threshold` argument can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation. The first opinion provides a clear automation strategy, while the second opinion acknowledges the potential for variations in kube-controller-manager deployment but still supports the automated approach. Given that the necessary information is accessible through Kubernetes resources, automation is both practical and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: kube-controller-manager - API/CLI Method: `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-controller-manager -o yaml` - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[].command` or `spec.containers[].args` for the `--terminated-pod-gc-threshold` argument - Pass Criteria: The `--terminated-pod-gc-threshold` flag is explicitly set and its value is less than or equal to the organization's defined maximum threshold. - Fail Criteria: The flag is missing (defaulting to 12500) or set to a value not aligned with the organization’s requirements.
