Then, edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameters. --peer-client-file=</path/to/peer-cert-file> --peer-key-file=</path/to/peer-key-file> Default Value: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, peer communication over TLS is not configured. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/
impact: etcd cluster peers would need to set up TLS for their communication.
justification: The task of verifying the configuration of etcd for TLS encryption can be effectively automated using Kubernetes CLI commands or scripts, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of this approach. The automation details provided are technically sound, leveraging `kubectl` to extract necessary information from the etcd pod specifications. Additionally, the task does not require manual inspection or SSH access, making it suitable for automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods (specifically etcd pods) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: The presence and validity of the `--peer-cert-file` and `--peer-key-file` arguments in the etcd pod specification. - Pass Criteria: Both `--peer-cert-file` and `--peer-key-file` are set to valid, non-empty file paths in the etcd pod spec. - Fail Criteria: Either flag is missing or empty in the etcd pod spec.

title: Ensure that the --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to true
description: etcd should be configured for peer authentication.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be accessible only by authenticated etcd peers in the etcd cluster. Impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that the --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to true. Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable.
remediation: Edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --peer-client-cert-auth=true Default Value: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to false. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/ 3. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#peer-client-cert- auth
impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. The task involves querying the etcd Pod specification, which is feasible through the Kubernetes CLI. The automation details provided are technically sound and align with the requirements of the compliance control.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -l component=etcd -o yaml` - Properties to Check: The presence of the `--peer-client-cert-auth=true` flag in the etcd container's command or args section. - Pass Criteria: The etcd pod spec includes `--peer-client-cert-auth=true`. - Fail Criteria: The flag is missing or set to `false`, or if the cluster is a single-node setup where this check is not applicable.

title: Ensure that the --peer-auto-tls argument is not set to true
description: Do not use automatically generated self-signed certificates for TLS connections between peers.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be accessible only by authenticated etcd peers in the etcd cluster. Hence, do not use self-signed certificates for authentication. Impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that if the --peer-auto-tls argument exists, it is not set to true. Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable.
remediation: Edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and either remove the --peer-auto-tls parameter or set it to false. --peer-auto-tls=false Default Value: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, --peer-auto-tls argument is set to false. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/ 3. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#peer-auto-tls
impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The control involves inspecting the configuration of etcd pods, which can be effectively checked without requiring manual intervention. The automation details provided are technically sound and align with best practices for Kubernetes compliance checks.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -l component=etcd -o yaml` - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[].command` or `spec.containers[].args` - Pass Criteria: The `--peer-auto-tls` flag is either not present or explicitly set to `false` (i.e., `--peer-auto-tls=false`). - Fail Criteria: The `--peer-auto-tls` flag is present and set to `true` (i.e., `--peer-auto-tls=true`).

title: Ensure that a unique Certificate Authority is used for etcd
description: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
rationale: etcd is a highly available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. Its access should be restricted to specifically designated clients and peers only. Authentication to etcd is based on whether the certificate presented was issued by a trusted certificate authority. There is no checking of certificate attributes such as common name or subject alternative name. As such, if any attackers were able to gain access to any certificate issued by the trusted certificate authority, they would be able to gain full access to the etcd database. Impact: Additional management of the certificates and keys for the dedicated certificate authority will be required.
audit: Review the CA used by the etcd environment and ensure that it does not match the CA certificate file used for the management of the overall Kubernetes cluster. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep etcd Note the file referenced by the --trusted-ca-file argument. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep apiserver Verify that the file referenced by the --client-ca-file for apiserver is different from the --trusted-ca-file used by etcd.
remediation: Follow the etcd documentation and create a dedicated certificate authority setup for the etcd service. Then, edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --trusted-ca-file=</path/to/ca-file> Default Value: By default, no etcd certificate is created and used. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html
impact: Additional management of the certificates and keys for the dedicated certificate authority will be required.
justification: The control requires direct inspection of static pod manifest files and the CA files they reference, which cannot be accessed through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions correctly identified the necessity of manual verification due to the host-level access required for this check. Therefore, an automated approach is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the master node(s) where etcd and the API server are running. 2. Locate the etcd static pod manifest file, typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml`. - Find the value for the `--trusted-ca-file` argument. 3. Locate the API server static pod manifest file, typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. - Find the value for the `--client-ca-file` argument. 4. Compare the file paths referenced by `--trusted-ca-file` (etcd) and `--client-ca-file` (API server). - Ensure they are different files. 5. Optionally, inspect the contents of both CA certificate files using the command: `openssl x509 -in <file> -text -noout` to confirm they are issued by different certificate authorities.

title: Client certificate authentication should not be used for users
description: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
rationale: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Kubernetes client certificate authentication does not allow for this due to a lack of support for certificate revocation. Impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
audit: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of Kubernetes client certificate authentication.
remediation: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of client certificates. Default Value: Client certificate authentication is enabled by default. Additional Information: The lack of certificate revocation was flagged up as a high risk issue in the recent Kubernetes security audit. Without this feature, client certificate authentication is not suitable for end users.
impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
justification: The nature of this compliance control requires a detailed examination of user authentication methods, which cannot be fully determined through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that the information needed to assess client certificate usage is not directly accessible, necessitating a manual review of kube-apiserver configurations and user kubeconfig files. Therefore, a manual approach is the most appropriate.
manual_summary: 1. **Review the kube-apiserver authentication configuration:** - Access the kube-apiserver manifest, typically located at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. - Check for the presence of the `--client-ca-file` flag, which indicates that client certificate authentication is enabled. 2. **Review user access mechanisms:** - Identify all users with access to the Kubernetes API by examining kubeconfig files, CI/CD pipelines, and automation scripts. - For each user, check the kubeconfig files for the presence of `client-certificate` and `client-key` fields under user entries, which indicate the use of client certificate authentication. 3. **Analyze kube-apiserver audit logs:** - If audit logging is enabled, review the logs for any entries that indicate client certificate usage. This may require configuring the audit policy to capture sufficient detail. 4. **Confirm compliance:** - Ensure that no users are utilizing client certificate authentication and that alternative mechanisms, such as OIDC, are in place.

title: Service account token authentication should not be used for users
description: Kubernetes provides service account tokens which are intended for use by workloads running in the Kubernetes cluster, for authentication to the API server. These tokens are not designed for use by end-users and do not provide for features such as revocation or expiry, making them insecure. A newer version of the feature (Bound service account token volumes) does introduce expiry but still does not allow for specific revocation.
rationale: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Service account token authentication does not allow for this due to the use of JWT tokens as an underlying technology. Impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
audit: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of service account token authentication.
remediation: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of service account tokens. Default Value: Service account token authentication is enabled by default.
impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
justification: The control requires a thorough review of user access patterns and authentication methods, which cannot be fully automated due to the limitations of the Kubernetes API in distinguishing between human users and service accounts. Both AI opinions agree on the necessity of a manual approach, emphasizing the need for detailed examination of audit logs and user configurations that are not directly accessible through automated means.
manual_summary: 1. **Review User Access:** - Gather a list of users with access to the Kubernetes API from your organization's identity provider or access management systems. - Ensure that you have the latest kubeconfig files for all users. 2. **Examine Kubernetes Audit Logs:** - If audit logging is enabled, access the audit logs. - Focus on the "user" field in the logs to identify authentication events. Look for any instances where service account tokens are being used by human users. 3. **Check Kubeconfig Files:** - Review the kubeconfig files for any entries that contain service account tokens. - Verify that users are not using service account tokens for authentication and are instead using supported mechanisms like OIDC or x509 certificates. 4. **Confirm Authentication Mechanisms:** - Ensure that all human users are authenticating via secure methods that allow for revocation and expiry, such as OIDC or x509 certificates.

title: Bootstrap token authentication should not be used for users
description: Kubernetes provides bootstrap tokens which are intended for use by new nodes joining the cluster These tokens are not designed for use by end-users they are specifically designed for the purpose of bootstrapping new nodes and not for general authentication
rationale: Bootstrap tokens are not intended for use as a general authentication mechanism and impose constraints on user and group naming that do not facilitate good RBAC design. They also cannot be used with MFA resulting in a weak authentication mechanism being available. Impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
audit: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of bootstrap token authentication.
remediation: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of bootstrap tokens. Default Value: Bootstrap token authentication is not enabled by default and requires an API server parameter to be set.
impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
justification: The primary check for the bootstrap token authentication can be effectively automated using kubectl commands to inspect the API server configuration. Both AI opinions support the automated approach, with the first opinion providing a clear method for checking the configuration flag. While the second opinion acknowledges the need for further manual investigation into user authentication, the core compliance control can be validated through automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: API Server - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: `--enable-bootstrap-token-auth` flag in the API server configuration - Pass Criteria: The flag is either absent or explicitly set to `false` (i.e., `--enable-bootstrap-token-auth=false`). - Fail Criteria: The flag is present and set to `true` (i.e., `--enable-bootstrap-token-auth=true`).

title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created
description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server. The --audit- policy-file flag must be set for this logging to be enabled.
rationale: Logging is an important detective control for all systems, to detect potential unauthorised access. Impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
audit: Run the following command on one of the cluster master nodes: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --audit-policy-file is set. Review the contents of the file specified and ensure that it contains a valid audit policy.
remediation: Create an audit policy file for your cluster. Default Value: Unless the --audit-policy-file flag is specified, no auditing will be carried out. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/
impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively assessed using Kubernetes API calls and `kubectl` commands to check for the presence of the `--audit-policy-file` flag in the kube-apiserver configuration. While there are some manual aspects regarding the validation of the audit policy file's contents, the primary check for the flag can be automated, making it feasible to implement an automated solution.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: kube-apiserver Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}'` - Properties to Check: The command output must include the `--audit-policy-file` flag with a valid file path. - Pass Criteria: All running kube-apiserver instances have the `--audit-policy-file` flag set to a valid file path. - Fail Criteria: If any kube-apiserver instance does not have the `--audit-policy-file` flag set, or if the specified file path does not exist or is invalid.

title: Ensure that the audit policy covers key security concerns
description: Ensure that the audit policy created for the cluster covers key security concerns.
rationale: Security audit logs should cover access and modification of key resources in the cluster, to enable them to form an effective part of a security environment. Impact: Increasing audit logging will consume resources on the nodes or other log destination.
audit: Review the audit policy provided for the cluster and ensure that it covers at least the following areas :- • Access to Secrets managed by the cluster. Care should be taken to only log Metadata for requests to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews, in order to avoid the risk of logging sensitive data. • Modification of pod and deployment objects. • Use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy and services/proxy. For most requests, minimally logging at the Metadata level is recommended (the most basic level of logging).
remediation: Consider modification of the audit policy in use on the cluster to include these items, at a minimum. Default Value: By default Kubernetes clusters do not log audit information. References: 1. https://github.com/k8scop/k8s-security- dashboard/blob/master/configs/kubernetes/adv-audit.yaml 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/#audit-policy 3. https://github.com/falcosecurity/falco/blob/master/examples/k8s_audit_config/aud it-policy.yaml 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/cluster/gce/gci/configure- helper.sh#L735
impact: Increasing audit logging will consume resources on the nodes or other log destination.
justification: The control requires a subjective review of the Kubernetes audit policy file, which cannot be fully validated through Kubernetes APIs or CLI tools. While some aspects could be assisted by automated tools, the critical evaluation of the policy's content, rules, and their order necessitates human judgment. Therefore, a manual approach is the most appropriate for ensuring compliance with the specified security concerns.
manual_summary: 1. Obtain the audit policy file in use by the API server. This can be found by checking the `--audit-policy-file` flag in the API server manifest, typically located at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. 2. Open the audit policy YAML file for review. 3. Verify that the policy includes rules that: - Log at least metadata for requests to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews to avoid logging sensitive data. - Log modifications (create, update, patch, delete) to pod and deployment objects. - Log the use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy, and services/proxy. 4. Ensure that the log level for these events is set to at least Metadata, confirming that sensitive data is not being logged. 5. Confirm that the policy is ordered correctly, as the order of rules can affect the logging behavior.

title: Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the kubelet service file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The kubelet service file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the kubelet service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Automated AAC auditing has been modified to allow CIS-CAT to input a variable for the <PATH>/<FILENAME> of the kubelet service config file. Please set $kubelet_service_config=<PATH> based on the file location on your system for example: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf To perform the audit manually: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf Default Value: By default, the kubelet service file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual approach due to the necessity of verifying file system permissions directly on the host operating system, which is not accessible through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps explicitly involve running OS-level commands that cannot be automated within the Kubernetes environment.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node. 2. Identify the path to the kubelet service file (commonly /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf or similar). 3. Run the following command to check the file permissions: ``` stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf ``` (Replace the path with the actual location if different.) 4. Verify that the output is 600 or more restrictive (e.g., 600, 400, 440, etc.). 5. If the permissions are more permissive than 600 (e.g., 640, 644), remediate by running: ``` chmod 600 /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf ``` 6. Repeat for all worker nodes in the cluster. 7. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and ensuring the output is 600 or more restrictive on all nodes.

title: Ensure that the kubelet service file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that the kubelet service file ownership is set to root:root.
rationale: The kubelet service file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the kubelet service in the worker node. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Automated AAC auditing has been modified to allow CIS-CAT to input a variable for the <PATH>/<FILENAME> of the kubelet service config file. Please set $kubelet_service_config=<PATH> based on the file location on your system for example: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf To perform the audit manually: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %U:%G /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chown root:root /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf Default Value: By default, kubelet service file ownership is set to root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control requires a manual approach due to the nature of the check, which involves verifying file ownership at the OS level. The kubelet service file ownership is not accessible through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl, necessitating direct access to the node's file system. Therefore, an automated solution is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. On each Kubernetes worker node, identify the location of the kubelet service configuration file (commonly found at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` or a similar path). 2. Execute the following command to check the file ownership: ``` stat -c %U:%G /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf ``` (Ensure to replace the path with the actual location of your kubelet service file if it differs.) 3. Verify that the output indicates: ``` root:root ``` 4. If the ownership is not set to `root:root`, remediate the issue by running: ``` chown root:root /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf ``` 5. Confirm compliance by re-running the `stat` command and ensuring the output is `root:root`.

title: If proxy kubeconfig file exists ensure permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: If kube-proxy is running, and if it is using a file-based kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The kube-proxy kubeconfig file controls various parameters of the kube-proxy service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. It is possible to run kube-proxy with the kubeconfig parameters configured as a Kubernetes ConfigMap instead of a file. In this case, there is no proxy kubeconfig file. Impact: None
audit: Find the kubeconfig file being used by kube-proxy by running the following command: ps -ef | grep kube-proxy If kube-proxy is running, get the kubeconfig file location from the --kubeconfig parameter. To perform the audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a <path><filename> Verify that a file is specified and it exists with permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 600 <proxy kubeconfig file> Default Value: By default, proxy file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/
impact: None
justification: The control requires checking file permissions on the kube-proxy kubeconfig file, which is an OS-level task not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. Both AI opinions agree that this process necessitates direct access to the filesystem of each worker node, confirming that a manual approach is necessary.
manual_summary: 1. On each worker node, identify the kube-proxy process and determine if it is running: - Run: `ps -ef | grep kube-proxy` 2. If kube-proxy is running, find the `--kubeconfig` flag to determine the path to the kubeconfig file. 3. Verify that the kubeconfig file exists at the specified path. 4. Check the file permissions: - Run: `stat -c %a <path-to-kubeconfig-file>` 5. Confirm that the permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive (e.g., 600, 400, 440). 6. If permissions are more permissive (e.g., 640, 644), remediate by running: - `chmod 600 <path-to-kubeconfig-file>` 7. Repeat for each worker node in the cluster.

title: If proxy kubeconfig file exists ensure ownership is set to root:root
description: If kube-proxy is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
rationale: The kubeconfig file for kube-proxy controls various parameters for the kube-proxy service in the worker node. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Find the kubeconfig file being used by kube-proxy by running the following command: ps -ef | grep kube-proxy If kube-proxy is running, get the kubeconfig file location from the --kubeconfig parameter. To perform the audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %U:%G <path><filename> Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chown root:root <proxy kubeconfig file> Default Value: By default, proxy file ownership is set to root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach must be manual due to the requirement of verifying file ownership on the host filesystem, which is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps necessitate direct interaction with the operating system of the worker nodes, confirming that automation is not feasible in this scenario.
manual_summary: 1. On each worker node, identify the kube-proxy process and determine the path to its kubeconfig file: - Run: `ps -ef | grep kube-proxy` - Locate the `--kubeconfig` parameter to find the file path. 2. Check the ownership of the kubeconfig file: - Run: `stat -c %U:%G <path-to-kubeconfig-file>` 3. Verify that the output shows `root:root` as the owner and group. 4. If the ownership is not `root:root`, remediate by running: - `chown root:root <path-to-kubeconfig-file>` 5. Repeat for each worker node where kube-proxy is running.

title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The kubelet.conf file is the kubeconfig file for the node, and controls various parameters that set the behavior and identity of the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None

impact: None

manual_summary: 1. SSH into each worker node in your Kubernetes cluster. 2. Identify the location of the kubelet.conf file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf, but may vary by installation). 3. Run the following command to check file permissions and ownership: ``` stat -c "%a %U:%G" /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf ``` 4. Verify that: - The permissions are 600 or more restrictive (i.e., only the owner has read and write permissions). - The ownership is root:root. 5. If the permissions are more permissive than 600 or the ownership is not root:root, remediate by running: ``` chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf ``` 6. Repeat for every worker node in the cluster.

title: Ensure that the certificate authorities file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the certificate authorities file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The certificate authorities file controls the authorities used to validate API requests. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None

manual_summary: 1. **Identify the kubelet process and its configuration:** - On each worker node, run: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` - Locate the `--client-ca-file` argument to determine the path to the certificate authorities file. 2. **Check the file permissions:** - On the node, run: ``` stat -c %a <path-to-client-ca-file> ``` - Replace `<path-to-client-ca-file>` with the actual file path found in step 1. 3. **Verify the permissions:** - Ensure the output is `600` or more restrictive (e.g., `400`). - Permissions such as `644` or higher (less restrictive) are NOT compliant. 4. **Confirm compliance:** - The file must be readable and writable only by the file owner (typically root or the kubelet user). - If the permissions are `600` or more restrictive, the node is compliant with this control.
