title: Minimize the admission of containers with allowPrivilegeEscalation
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true. Allowing this right can lead to a process running a container getting more rights than it started with. It's important to note that these rights are still constrained by the overall container sandbox, and this setting does not relate to the use of privileged containers.
rationale: A container running with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true may have processes that can gain more privileges than their parent. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to allow privilege escalation. The option exists (and is defaulted to true) to permit setuid binaries to run. If you have need to run containers which use setuid binaries or require privilege escalation, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which allow privilege escalation. To fetch hostNetwork from each pod. get pods -A -o=jsonpath=$'{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.name}: {@..securityContext}\n{end}'
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of conatiners with securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: true Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on contained process ability to escalate privileges, within the context of the container. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the classification as automated, and the tasks can be effectively executed using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The automation details provided are technically sound and leverage the capabilities of Kubernetes to check for compliance regarding the `allowPrivilegeEscalation` flag. The approach is feasible and aligns with Kubernetes best practices.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: `securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation` - Pass Criteria: All namespaces with user workloads have enforced Pod Security Admission policies at `baseline` or `restricted` that disallow `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`, and no running pods have containers with `securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace does not have the required Pod Security Admission policies, or any running pod has containers with `securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`.

title: Minimize the admission of root containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run as the root user.
rationale: Containers may run as any Linux user. Containers which run as the root user, whilst constrained by Container Runtime security features still have a escalated likelihood of container breakout. Ideally, all containers should run as a defined non-UID 0 user. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit root containers. If you need to run root containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy restricts the use of root containers by setting MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0.
remediation: Create a policy for each namespace in the cluster, ensuring that either MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0, is set. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of root containers and if a User is not specified in the image, the container will run as root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The audit steps outlined in the control data can be executed programmatically, allowing for efficient and consistent compliance checks across multiple namespaces. The automation details provided are comprehensive and leverage existing Kubernetes features, making this approach both feasible and practical.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA) and PodSecurityPolicy (PSP) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: - For PSA: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn` - For PSP: `runAsUser.rule` should be `MustRunAsNonRoot` or `MustRunAs` with UID ranges excluding 0. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace must have at least one enforced policy (via PSA or PSP) that restricts the admission of root containers. - Fail Criteria: If any namespace lacks an enforced policy that meets the above criteria, it indicates non-compliance.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability
description: Do not generally permit containers with the potentially dangerous NET_RAW capability.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. By default this can include potentially dangerous capabilities. With Docker as the container runtime the NET_RAW capability is enabled which may be misused by malicious containers. Ideally, all containers should drop this capability. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers with the NET_RAW capability. If you need to run containers with this capability, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy disallows the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with the NET_RAW capability. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
justification: The audit approach can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of this method. The first opinion provides a comprehensive automation strategy, while the second opinion acknowledges the potential for automation and suggests improvements. Given the technical capabilities of Kubernetes, this control can be efficiently monitored and enforced through automated checks.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA), Pod Security Policies (PSP), OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: - PSA labels: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn` - PSP rules (if applicable) - OPA/Gatekeeper constraints - Kyverno policies - Pass Criteria: Every namespace with user workloads must have at least one enforced policy that denies pods with the NET_RAW capability. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace with user workloads lacking an enforced policy that restricts the NET_RAW capability.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with added capabilities
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities assigned beyond the default set.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities outside this set can be added to containers which could expose them to risks of container breakout attacks. There should be at least one policy defined which prevents containers with capabilities beyond the default set from launching. If you need to run containers with additional capabilities, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities outwith the default set will not be permitted.
audit: Ensure that allowedCapabilities is not present in policies for the cluster unless it is set to an empty array. get pods -A -o=jsonpath=$'{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.name}: {@..securityContext}\n{end}'
remediation: Ensure that allowedCapabilities is not present in policies for the cluster unless it is set to an empty array. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on adding capabilities to containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities outwith the default set will not be permitted.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools. The compliance check can be fully executed by enumerating and validating Pod Security Policies (PSPs), Pod Security Admission (PSA) policies, and third-party admission controllers, which are all accessible via `kubectl`. This aligns with the control's requirements and the capabilities of Kubernetes.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Policies (PSPs), Pod Security Admission (PSA) - API/CLI Method: - For PSPs: `kubectl get psp -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, allowedCapabilities: .spec.allowedCapabilities}'` - For PSA: `kubectl get ns --show-labels` - Properties to Check: `allowedCapabilities` field in PSPs and namespace labels for PSA - Pass Criteria: No policy in the cluster allows additional capabilities (i.e., all `allowedCapabilities` fields are empty or absent, or PSA restricted is enforced). - Fail Criteria: Any policy with `allowedCapabilities` present containing values, or if PSA restricted is not enforced.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with capabilities assigned
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities are parts of the rights generally granted on a Linux system to the root user. In many cases applications running in containers do not require any capabilities to operate, so from the perspective of the principal of least privilege use of capabilities should be minimized. Impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy requires that capabilities are dropped by all containers.
remediation: Review the use of capabilities in applications running on your cluster. Where a namespace contains applications which do not require any Linux capabilities to operate consider adding a policy which forbids the admission of containers which do not drop all capabilities. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with additional capabilities References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
justification: The audit process for this compliance control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of an automated approach. The first opinion provides a comprehensive automation strategy that aligns with the control's requirements, and the second opinion supports the automation classification while highlighting the need for careful policy inspection, which can still be incorporated into an automated framework.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get ns`, `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: - For namespaces: Check for the presence of `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted` label. - For PodSecurityPolicies (if applicable): Ensure `requiredDropCapabilities` is set to `ALL` or `allowedCapabilities` is empty. - For OPA/Gatekeeper policies: Inspect for rules denying non-empty `securityContext.capabilities.add`. - Pass Criteria: At least one enforced policy (PSA, PSP, or admission controller policy) per namespace that requires all containers to drop all capabilities. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace without an enforced policy that meets the above criteria.

title: Minimize the admission of Windows HostProcess Containers
description: Do not generally permit Windows containers to be run with the hostProcess flag set to true.
rationale: A Windows container making use of the hostProcess flag can interact with the underlying Windows cluster node. As per the Kubernetes documentation, this provides "privileged access" to the Windows node. Where Windows containers are used inside a Kubernetes cluster, there should be at least one admission control policy which does not permit hostProcess Windows containers. If you need to run Windows containers which require hostProcess, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostProcess containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostProcess containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostProcess containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/create-hostprocess-pod/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated, as it involves querying and verifying configurations across namespaces using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools. The task is well-suited for automation, given that it requires checking for specific admission policies and configurations that can be programmatically validated.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission Policies - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: - `spec.containers[*].securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true` - Admission policies that restrict the use of hostProcess containers - Pass Criteria: Every namespace with user workloads must have a policy that denies pods with `windowsOptions.hostProcess: true`, unless explicitly allowed for tightly controlled service accounts. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace lacking such a policy, or where a pod with `hostProcess: true` can be admitted without restrictions.

title: Minimize the admission of HostPath volumes
description: Do not generally admit containers which make use of hostPath volumes.
rationale: A container which mounts a hostPath volume as part of its specification will have access to the filesystem of the underlying cluster node. The use of hostPath volumes may allow containers access to privileged areas of the node filesystem. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to mount hostPath volumes. If you need to run containers which require hostPath volumes, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a spefific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers with hostPath volumes.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPath volumes. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPath volumes. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a spefific policy.
justification: The audit approach is classified as Automated because both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands to check for compliance with the control regarding hostPath volumes. The proposed automation details effectively leverage Kubernetes capabilities to enumerate namespaces and verify policy enforcement, making it suitable for this compliance control.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA), PodSecurityPolicies (PSP), third-party admission controllers - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get ns --show-labels` to check PSA labels - `kubectl get psp -o yaml` (if PSPs are enabled) - For OPA/Kyverno: `kubectl get constraints` or `kubectl get clusterpolicy` - Properties to Check: - Presence and enforcement mode of PSA labels - Spec.volumes not including "hostPath" or minimal allowedHostPaths in PSPs - At least one policy denying hostPath volumes in third-party controllers - Pass Criteria: Every namespace with user workloads must have enforced policies that disallow hostPath volumes. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace allowing hostPath volumes without appropriate restrictions.

title: Minimize the admission of containers which use HostPorts
description: Do not generally permit containers which require the use of HostPorts.
rationale: Host ports connect containers directly to the host's network. This can bypass controls such as network policy. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers which require the use of HostPorts. If you need to run containers which require HostPorts, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which have hostPort sections.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPort sections. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of HostPorts. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 5.3 Network Policies and CNI
impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The audit can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands to enumerate namespaces, check for Pod Security Admission policies, and validate the presence of `hostPort` configurations. Both AI opinions support automation, with the first opinion providing a detailed approach that aligns with the audit steps outlined in the compliance control data. The second opinion acknowledges the automated nature of the checks while suggesting some manual verification, but the core audit process can be fully automated.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Deployments, ReplicaSets, DaemonSets, StatefulSets, Jobs - API/CLI Method: Kubernetes API and kubectl commands - Properties to Check: - `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` and `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version` labels - `spec.containers[*].ports[*].hostPort`, `spec.initContainers[*].ports[*].hostPort`, `spec.ephemeralContainers[*].ports[*].hostPort` - Pass Criteria: - Each namespace with user workloads has a Pod Security Admission policy that restricts the use of `hostPort`. - No pods or workload resources define `hostPort` unless explicitly allowed by policy. - Fail Criteria: - Presence of any `hostPort` in workloads that are not in namespaces with an explicit policy allowing it.

title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None
audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
remediation: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
impact: None
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
justification: The control requires a verification process that involves reviewing external documentation of the CNI plugin to confirm support for Ingress and Egress network policies. Both AI opinions agree that Kubernetes APIs and kubectl do not provide the necessary information to determine the capabilities of the CNI plugin directly, necessitating a manual review of documentation.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the CNI plugin in use: - Run: `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` and look for pods with names like `calico`, `weave`, `flannel`, `cilium`, etc. - Alternatively, inspect the CNI configuration file on a node (typically at `/etc/cni/net.d/`), if you have access. 2. Determine the exact version of the CNI plugin deployed (from pod image tags or CNI config). 3. Review the official documentation for the identified CNI plugin and version. - Confirm that it supports both Ingress and Egress Kubernetes Network Policies. - Example: For Calico and Cilium, both are supported; for Flannel, NetworkPolicy is not supported unless used with Calico. 4. Document your findings, including the CNI plugin name, version, and a reference to the documentation confirming support for both policy types.

title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace. Impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them. Default Value: By default, network policies are not created. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/networkpolicies/ 2. https://octetz.com/posts/k8s-network-policy-apis 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/declare-network-policy/
impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The audit steps provided in the compliance control data can be effectively executed through automation, allowing for a systematic check of NetworkPolicy objects across namespaces. The presence of Network Policies can be validated without requiring manual intervention, making automation the most efficient approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: NetworkPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace>` - Properties to Check: Existence of at least one NetworkPolicy object in each non-system namespace - Pass Criteria: Every non-system namespace has at least one NetworkPolicy defined - Fail Criteria: One or more non-system namespaces have zero NetworkPolicy objects

title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
rationale: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs. Impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
audit: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
remediation: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables. Default Value: By default, secrets are not defined References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of an automated approach. The proposed automation details accurately outline how to identify the use of secrets as environment variables, which can be efficiently executed using `kubectl` and `jq`. This aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes to programmatically inspect resource configurations.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Deployments, ReplicaSets, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs - API/CLI Method: kubectl get all --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: Inspect `spec.template.spec.containers[].env[]` and `spec.template.spec.initContainers[].env[]` for `valueFrom.secretKeyRef` - Pass Criteria: No containers in any Pod-spec-based resource use `secretKeyRef` in `env` or `envFrom`. All secrets are mounted as files (volumes) instead. - Fail Criteria: Any container in any resource uses `secretKeyRef` in `env` or `envFrom`.

title: Consider external secret storage
description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
rationale: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments. Impact: None
audit: Review your secrets management implementation.
remediation: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution. Default Value: By default, no external secret management is configured.
impact: None
justification: The control focuses on architectural decisions regarding the use of external secret management solutions, which cannot be directly verified through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that the assessment requires human judgment and documentation review, as it involves evaluating the integration of external systems rather than checking configurations that are exposed via Kubernetes resources.
manual_summary: 1. Review the cluster documentation and architecture diagrams to determine if an external secrets management solution is integrated with Kubernetes. 2. Identify which external secrets management system (if any) is in use (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, etc.). 3. Verify that Kubernetes workloads retrieve secrets from the external system (e.g., via CSI Secret Store driver, external secrets operator, or custom integration), rather than using native Kubernetes Secret objects. 4. Confirm that the external secrets solution enforces authentication for secret access, provides auditing of secret access and usage, and encrypts secrets at rest and in transit. 5. Check if the solution supports secret rotation and verify the configuration for compliance with security best practices.

title: Configure Image Provenance using ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller
description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
rationale: Kubernetes supports plugging in provenance rules to accept or reject the images in your deployments. You could configure such rules to ensure that only approved images are deployed in the cluster. Impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that image provenance is configured as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup image provenance. Default Value: By default, image provenance is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#imagepolicywebhook 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design- proposals/image-provenance.md 3. https://hub.docker.com/r/dnurmi/anchore-toolbox/ 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/22888
impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
justification: While there are aspects of the ImagePolicyWebhook configuration that can be checked through automated means, the comprehensive review of the webhook configuration, policy logic, and the specific provenance rules requires subjective analysis that cannot be fully automated. The audit steps necessitate a detailed understanding of the organization's policies and the specific configurations that are not exposed via Kubernetes APIs.
manual_summary: 1. Access the Kubernetes master node(s) and review the API server startup arguments (e.g., via systemd unit files or manifest files in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/). 2. Verify that the `--admission-control` or `--enable-admission-plugins` flag includes `ImagePolicyWebhook`. 3. Locate the ImagePolicyWebhook configuration file specified by the `--admission-control-config-file` or `--admission-control-config` flags. 4. Review the webhook configuration to ensure it is set up to enforce image provenance policies (e.g., only allowing images from approved registries, requiring signed images, etc.). 5. Examine the policy logic and rules to confirm they meet your organization's provenance requirements. 6. Review a sample of pod definitions to ensure that only approved images are being used in deployments.

title: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
rationale: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default. You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users. Impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
audit: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them. Default Value: By default, Kubernetes starts with 4 initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with- objects/namespaces/#viewing-namespaces 2. http://blog.kubernetes.io/2016/08/security-best-practices-kubernetes- deployment.html 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-node/589- efficient-node-heartbeats
impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as both AI opinions confirm the feasibility of automation. The checks for namespace distribution and the presence of workloads in non-default namespaces can be automated without requiring manual interpretation. Therefore, an automated approach is appropriate for this control.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces, Pods, Deployments, Services - API/CLI Method: kubectl get namespaces, kubectl get all --all-namespaces - Properties to Check: - Existence of at least one non-default namespace. - Workloads (Pods, Deployments, etc.) present in at least one non-default namespace. - Only expected system namespaces (default, kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) exist. - Pass Criteria: - At least one non-system, non-default namespace exists. - Workloads are present in at least one non-default namespace. - Fail Criteria: - Only system namespaces and the default namespace exist, or all workloads are deployed only in the default namespace.

title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container. Impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault Default Value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ 2. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/
impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be effectively checked using Kubernetes APIs and the `kubectl` command-line tool. The ability to enumerate Pods and inspect their configurations programmatically supports an automated approach. The automation details provided are technically sound and feasible within the Kubernetes environment.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: `spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type` at both Pod and container levels - Pass Criteria: Every Pod (and/or its containers, if Pod-level is unset) must have `seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault` - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container missing the `seccompProfile` field, or with `type` not set to `RuntimeDefault` (e.g., set to `Unconfined` or custom profile), is non-compliant.

title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context. Impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers. Default Value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/security-context/ 2. https://learn.cisecurity.org/benchmarks
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl`. The task involves querying Pod resources and checking for specific fields, which is well-suited for automation. The provided automation details are technically sound and align with the requirements of the compliance control.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: .spec.securityContext at the Pod level and .securityContext for each container - Pass Criteria: At least one securityContext field (either at the Pod or container level) is defined for each Pod and its containers. - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container is found without a securityContext field defined.

title: The default namespace should not be used
description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
rationale: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources. Impact: None
audit: Run this command to list objects in default namespace kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default The only entries there should be system managed resources such as the kubernetes service
remediation: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace. Default Value: Unless a namespace is specific on object creation, the default namespace will be used
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this compliance control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and the `kubectl` CLI. The audit steps outlined in the control data can be executed programmatically to check for user-created resources in the default namespace, making it suitable for an automated approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaced resources (e.g., Pods, Deployments, ConfigMaps, Secrets) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name` followed by `kubectl get <resource> -n default` - Properties to Check: Ensure only system-managed resources (like the `kubernetes` service) exist in the default namespace. - Pass Criteria: Only system-managed resources are present in the default namespace; no user-created resources are found. - Fail Criteria: Any user-created resource (other than the default `kubernetes` service) is found in the default namespace.

title: Ensure that the API server pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual approach due to the necessity of OS-level access to check file permissions, which cannot be accomplished through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps explicitly involve running shell commands on the control plane node, further solidifying the need for manual verification.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Kubernetes control plane node where the kube-apiserver is running. 2. Run the following command to check the permissions of the kube-apiserver pod specification file (adjust the path if your file is located elsewhere): ``` stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 3. Review the output. The permissions should be 600 or more restrictive (e.g., 600, 400, 440, etc.). 4. If the permissions are more permissive than 600 (e.g., 640, 644, 660, 666, etc.), this is non-compliant. 5. To remediate, run: ``` chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 6. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and verifying the output is 600 or more restrictive.

title: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root.
rationale: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file ownership is set to root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual audit approach due to the necessity of checking file ownership on the host filesystem, which is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. The audit steps explicitly require OS-level access, confirming that automation is not feasible in this scenario.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node. 2. Run the following command to check the ownership of the kube-apiserver manifest file: ``` stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` (Replace the path if your kube-apiserver manifest is in a different location.) 3. Verify that the output is: ``` root:root ``` 4. If the ownership is not root:root, remediate by running: ``` chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 5. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and ensuring the output is root:root.

title: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The controller manager pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the Controller Manager on the master node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-controller-manager.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/

Run the following command to check file ownership: ``` stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf ``` 4. Verify that the output is: ``` root:root ``` 5. If the ownership is not root:root, remediate by running: ``` chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf ``` 6. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and ensuring the output is root:root.

title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root.
rationale: Kubernetes makes use of a number of certificates as part of its operation. You should set the ownership of the directory containing the PKI information and all files in that directory to maintain their integrity. The directory and files should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/ Verify that the ownership of all files and directories in this hierarchy is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chown -R root:root /etc/kubernetes/pki/ Default Value: By default, the /etc/kubernetes/pki/ directory and all of the files and directories contained within it, are set to be owned by the root user. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: The control requires verification of file ownership on the host filesystem, which is not accessible through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that this check must be performed manually on the control plane node(s) since the ownership information is not exposed via Kubernetes-native tools. While the second opinion suggests the potential for automation using configuration management tools, the fundamental requirement for direct filesystem access remains, which necessitates a manual approach.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node. 2. Run the following command to recursively list ownership of all files and directories under the PKI directory: ``` ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/ ``` 3. Review the output to verify that every file and directory is owned by user root and group root. 4. Confirm compliance if all files and directories under /etc/kubernetes/pki/ have ownership set to root:root. If any file or directory is not owned by root:root, this is non-compliant and must be remediated.

title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI certificate file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that Kubernetes PKI certificate files have permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
rationale: Kubernetes makes use of a number of certificate files as part of the operation of its components. The permissions on these files should be set to 644 or more restrictive to protect their integrity and confidentiality. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c '%a' /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive. or ls -l /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt Verify -rw------
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod -R 644 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt Default Value: By default, the certificates used by Kubernetes are set to have permissions of 644 References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual audit approach due to the necessity of direct access to the host operating system to check file permissions, which cannot be performed through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps explicitly involve running OS-level commands, confirming that automation is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node. 2. Run the following command to list the permissions of all PKI certificate files: ``` stat -c '%n %a' /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt ``` or ``` ls -l /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt ``` 3. For each listed certificate file, verify that the permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive (i.e., the numeric mode is 644, 640, 600, etc.). 4. Confirm compliance if all certificate files have permissions of 644 or more restrictive. If any file has less restrictive permissions (e.g., 666, 664, 777), this is non-compliant.

title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI key file permissions are set to 600

rationale: Kubernetes makes use of a number of key files as part of the operation of its components. The permissions on these files should be set to 600 to protect their integrity and confidentiality. Impact: None
