title: Ensure that the --client-cert-auth argument is set to true
description: Enable client authentication on etcd service.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should not be available to unauthenticated clients. You should enable the client authentication via valid certificates to secure the access to the etcd service. Impact: All clients attempting to access the etcd server will require a valid client certificate.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that the --client-cert-auth argument is set to true.
remediation: Edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --client-cert-auth="true" Default Value: By default, the etcd service can be queried by unauthenticated clients. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/ 3. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#client-cert-auth
impact: All clients attempting to access the etcd server will require a valid client certificate.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be effectively automated by querying the etcd static Pod manifest using `kubectl` or by directly inspecting the manifest file. The rationale for enabling client authentication on etcd is critical for securing sensitive data, and the automation details provided are technically sound and feasible within Kubernetes capabilities.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: etcd Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -o yaml` or direct file inspection - Properties to Check: Ensure that the `--client-cert-auth` argument is set to `true` in the etcd Pod specification. - Pass Criteria: The etcd manifest must explicitly include `--client-cert-auth=true`. - Fail Criteria: The flag is missing, set to `false`, or not present at all.

title: Ensure that the --auto-tls argument is not set to true
description: Do not use self-signed certificates for TLS.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should not be available to unauthenticated clients. You should enable the client authentication via valid certificates to secure the access to the etcd service. Impact: Clients will not be able to use self-signed certificates for TLS.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that if the --auto-tls argument exists, it is not set to true.
remediation: Edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and either remove the --auto-tls parameter or set it to false. --auto-tls=false Default Value: By default, --auto-tls is set to false. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/ 3. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#auto-tls
impact: Clients will not be able to use self-signed certificates for TLS.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl` commands without requiring manual inspection or host-level access. The proposed automation details are technically sound and align with the requirements of the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark. The ability to query the etcd pod specification programmatically supports the automated approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods (specifically etcd Pods) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=etcd -o json` - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[].args` for the presence of `--auto-tls` - Pass Criteria: The `--auto-tls` flag is either not present or explicitly set to `false` (i.e., `--auto-tls=false`). - Fail Criteria: The `--auto-tls` flag is present and set to `true` (i.e., `--auto-tls=true`).

title: Ensure that the --peer-cert-file and --peer-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for peer connections.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be encrypted in transit and also amongst peers in the etcd clusters. Impact: etcd cluster peers would need to set up TLS for their communication.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that the --peer-cert-file and --peer-key-file arguments are set as appropriate. Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable.
remediation: Follow the etcd service documentation and configure peer TLS encryption as appropriate for your etcd cluster. Then, edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameters. --peer-client-file=</path/to/peer-cert-file> --peer-key-file=</path/to/peer-key-file> Default Value: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, peer communication over TLS is not configured. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/
impact: etcd cluster peers would need to set up TLS for their communication.
justification: The task of verifying the `--peer-cert-file` and `--peer-key-file` arguments can be effectively automated using Kubernetes CLI commands, as both AI opinions support the feasibility of automation. The first opinion provides a clear method for checking the configuration, while the second opinion acknowledges the appropriateness of using `kubectl` for this task. Given that the necessary properties can be accessed through Kubernetes APIs, automation is both possible and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}'` - Properties to Check: Presence and non-empty values of `--peer-cert-file` and `--peer-key-file` - Pass Criteria: Both `--peer-cert-file` and `--peer-key-file` arguments are present and set to non-empty values in the etcd pod specification. - Fail Criteria: Either argument is missing or set to an empty value.

title: Ensure that the --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to true
description: etcd should be configured for peer authentication.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be accessible only by authenticated etcd peers in the etcd cluster. Impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that the --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to true. Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable.
remediation: Edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --peer-client-cert-auth=true Default Value: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to false. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/ 3. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#peer-client-cert- auth
impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. The necessary checks can be performed programmatically by retrieving the etcd pod manifest and verifying the presence of the `--peer-client-cert-auth=true` argument, which aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -o yaml` or direct file access to `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml` - Properties to Check: The `command` or `args` field of the etcd container for the presence of `--peer-client-cert-auth=true` - Pass Criteria: The etcd container is started with `--peer-client-cert-auth=true` - Fail Criteria: The etcd container is started with `--peer-client-cert-auth=false` or the flag is missing (defaults to false)

title: Ensure that the --peer-auto-tls argument is not set to true
description: Do not use automatically generated self-signed certificates for TLS connections between peers.
rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be accessible only by authenticated etcd peers in the etcd cluster. Hence, do not use self-signed certificates for authentication. Impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that if the --peer-auto-tls argument exists, it is not set to true. Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable.
remediation: Edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and either remove the --peer-auto-tls parameter or set it to false. --peer-auto-tls=false Default Value: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, --peer-auto-tls argument is set to false. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/ 3. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#peer-auto-tls
impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
justification: The task of checking the `--peer-auto-tls` argument in the etcd Pod specification can be effectively automated using `kubectl` commands to query the Kubernetes API. Both AI opinions support the automated classification, and the first opinion provides a clear method for retrieving and validating the necessary information. The second opinion acknowledges the appropriateness of automation, despite some minor critiques, which do not undermine the overall feasibility of an automated approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -l component=etcd -o yaml` - Properties to Check: `command` or `args` fields for the presence of `--peer-auto-tls` - Pass Criteria: The `--peer-auto-tls` flag is either not present (default is `false`), or explicitly set to `false`. - Fail Criteria: The `--peer-auto-tls` flag is present and set to `true`.

title: Ensure that a unique Certificate Authority is used for etcd
description: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
rationale: etcd is a highly available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. Its access should be restricted to specifically designated clients and peers only. Authentication to etcd is based on whether the certificate presented was issued by a trusted certificate authority. There is no checking of certificate attributes such as common name or subject alternative name. As such, if any attackers were able to gain access to any certificate issued by the trusted certificate authority, they would be able to gain full access to the etcd database. Impact: Additional management of the certificates and keys for the dedicated certificate authority will be required.
audit: Review the CA used by the etcd environment and ensure that it does not match the CA certificate file used for the management of the overall Kubernetes cluster. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep etcd Note the file referenced by the --trusted-ca-file argument. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep apiserver Verify that the file referenced by the --client-ca-file for apiserver is different from the --trusted-ca-file used by etcd.
remediation: Follow the etcd documentation and create a dedicated certificate authority setup for the etcd service. Then, edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --trusted-ca-file=</path/to/ca-file> Default Value: By default, no etcd certificate is created and used. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/security.html
impact: Additional management of the certificates and keys for the dedicated certificate authority will be required.
justification: The control requires verifying that the Certificate Authority (CA) used for etcd is different from the CA used for the Kubernetes API server, which necessitates access to the host filesystem where the static pod manifests are stored. Both AI opinions agree that this check cannot be fully automated using Kubernetes APIs or kubectl, as the necessary configurations are not exposed through these interfaces. Therefore, a manual approach is the only viable option.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the master node(s) where etcd and kube-apiserver are running. 2. Locate the etcd static pod manifest, typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml`. - Find the value for the `--trusted-ca-file` argument. 3. Locate the kube-apiserver static pod manifest, typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. - Find the value for the `--client-ca-file` argument. 4. Compare the file paths referenced by `--trusted-ca-file` (etcd) and `--client-ca-file` (kube-apiserver). - Ensure they point to different CA certificate files. 5. (Optional but recommended) Inspect the contents of both CA certificate files to confirm they are indeed different CAs (not just different file names). - Use a command like `openssl x509 -in <ca-file> -noout -issuer -subject` to compare the issuer and subject fields.

title: Client certificate authentication should not be used for users
description: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
rationale: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Kubernetes client certificate authentication does not allow for this due to a lack of support for certificate revocation. Impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
audit: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of Kubernetes client certificate authentication.
remediation: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of client certificates. Default Value: Client certificate authentication is enabled by default. Additional Information: The lack of certificate revocation was flagged up as a high risk issue in the recent Kubernetes security audit. Without this feature, client certificate authentication is not suitable for end users.
impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
justification: The control requires a thorough review of user authentication methods, specifically ensuring that client certificate authentication is not being used. Both AI opinions agree that while some aspects could be partially automated, the lack of direct Kubernetes API support for listing users authenticating via client certificates necessitates a manual review process. The complexity of user access management and the need to inspect external identity provider configurations further support the manual approach.
manual_summary: 1. Identify all users with access to the Kubernetes cluster. - Review your organization's access management documentation or identity provider configuration to compile a list of users. 2. Review the Kubernetes API server authentication configuration. - On each control plane node, inspect the API server startup arguments (e.g., `--client-ca-file`) to determine if client certificates are enabled. - Confirm that OIDC or another revocable authentication mechanism is configured for user authentication. 3. Examine how kubeconfig files are provisioned to users. - Check if user kubeconfig files reference client certificate/key pairs (client-certificate and client-key fields). 4. Verify that no users are utilizing client certificate authentication. - Cross-reference the identified users with the kubeconfig files to ensure none are using client certificates for authentication.

title: Service account token authentication should not be used for users
description: Kubernetes provides service account tokens which are intended for use by workloads running in the Kubernetes cluster, for authentication to the API server. These tokens are not designed for use by end-users and do not provide for features such as revocation or expiry, making them insecure. A newer version of the feature (Bound service account token volumes) does introduce expiry but still does not allow for specific revocation.
rationale: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Service account token authentication does not allow for this due to the use of JWT tokens as an underlying technology. Impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
audit: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of service account token authentication.
remediation: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of service account tokens. Default Value: Service account token authentication is enabled by default.
impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
justification: The control requires a thorough review of user access methods and the verification that human users are not using service account tokens for authentication. Both AI opinions agree that this process cannot be fully automated due to the limitations of Kubernetes APIs in distinguishing between human users and service accounts. The manual approach is necessary to ensure a comprehensive audit, including reviewing audit logs and organizational practices.
manual_summary: 1. Review all configured authentication methods for the Kubernetes API server, including OIDC, client certificates, webhook, and service account tokens. 2. Obtain a list of all users with access to the Kubernetes cluster from RBAC bindings, identity provider, or organizational user directory. 3. Review Kubernetes audit logs (if enabled) for evidence of service account token usage by human users. Look specifically for token-based authentication attempts from non-workload sources. 4. Interview cluster administrators or review documentation to confirm that human users are not issued or using service account tokens for API access. 5. Ensure that all human users authenticate via approved methods such as OIDC or client certificates, and document the findings.

title: Bootstrap token authentication should not be used for users
description: Kubernetes provides bootstrap tokens which are intended for use by new nodes joining the cluster These tokens are not designed for use by end-users they are specifically designed for the purpose of bootstrapping new nodes and not for general authentication
rationale: Bootstrap tokens are not intended for use as a general authentication mechanism and impose constraints on user and group naming that do not facilitate good RBAC design. They also cannot be used with MFA resulting in a weak authentication mechanism being available. Impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
audit: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of bootstrap token authentication.
remediation: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of bootstrap tokens. Default Value: Bootstrap token authentication is not enabled by default and requires an API server parameter to be set.
impact: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively assessed using automated methods through Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. Both AI opinions recognize the feasibility of automation for checking the API server configuration and enumerating ServiceAccount tokens. While there is a manual aspect regarding RBAC roles, the core compliance check can still be automated, making this the most efficient approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: API Server - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: - The presence and value of the `--enable-bootstrap-token-auth` flag in the API server configuration. - Enumeration of secrets of type `bootstrap.kubernetes.io/token`. - Pass Criteria: - The `--enable-bootstrap-token-auth` flag is either absent or explicitly set to `false`. - No users are authenticated via bootstrap tokens; only node bootstrapping uses such tokens. - Fail Criteria: - The `--enable-bootstrap-token-auth` flag is present and set to `true`. - Any RBAC roles or bindings that grant user access to use bootstrap tokens for authentication.

title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created
description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server. The --audit- policy-file flag must be set for this logging to be enabled.
rationale: Logging is an important detective control for all systems, to detect potential unauthorised access. Impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
audit: Run the following command on one of the cluster master nodes: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --audit-policy-file is set. Review the contents of the file specified and ensure that it contains a valid audit policy.
remediation: Create an audit policy file for your cluster. Default Value: Unless the --audit-policy-file flag is specified, no auditing will be carried out. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/
impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
justification: The control requires verification of both the presence of the `--audit-policy-file` flag and the contents of the specified audit policy file. While the presence of the flag can be checked using Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands, the actual contents of the audit policy file necessitate direct access to the file system, which cannot be automated through Kubernetes APIs. Therefore, a manual approach is necessary to ensure compliance.
manual_summary: 1. SSH to a Kubernetes control plane node (master node). 2. Locate the kube-apiserver manifest file (commonly at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`) or inspect the running process arguments using: `ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver` 3. Verify that the `--audit-policy-file` flag is present in the kube-apiserver command line or manifest. 4. Note the path specified for the audit policy file (e.g., `/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml`). 5. Open and review the contents of the audit policy file at the specified path. 6. Confirm that the file contains a valid Kubernetes audit policy (refer to the official documentation: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/). 7. Ensure that the audit policy is minimal and does not generate excessive log volume.

title: Ensure that the audit policy covers key security concerns
description: Ensure that the audit policy created for the cluster covers key security concerns.
rationale: Security audit logs should cover access and modification of key resources in the cluster, to enable them to form an effective part of a security environment. Impact: Increasing audit logging will consume resources on the nodes or other log destination.
audit: Review the audit policy provided for the cluster and ensure that it covers at least the following areas :- • Access to Secrets managed by the cluster. Care should be taken to only log Metadata for requests to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews, in order to avoid the risk of logging sensitive data. • Modification of pod and deployment objects. • Use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy and services/proxy. For most requests, minimally logging at the Metadata level is recommended (the most basic level of logging).
remediation: Consider modification of the audit policy in use on the cluster to include these items, at a minimum. Default Value: By default Kubernetes clusters do not log audit information. References: 1. https://github.com/k8scop/k8s-security- dashboard/blob/master/configs/kubernetes/adv-audit.yaml 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/#audit-policy 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/cluster/gce/gci/configure- helper.sh#L735
impact: Increasing audit logging will consume resources on the nodes or other log destination.
justification: The review of the Kubernetes audit policy requires direct access to the audit policy file on the master node, which is not exposed through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that a subjective review is necessary to ensure the audit policy covers key security concerns, making a manual approach the only viable option.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the audit policy file in use: - On the Kubernetes master node(s), check the API server startup arguments (e.g., `--audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml`). 2. Access the referenced audit policy YAML file on the master node. 3. Review the audit policy rules and ensure they include: - Logging of access to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews at the Metadata level only. - Logging of modifications to pod and deployment objects. - Logging of use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy, and services/proxy. - For most requests, ensure at least Metadata level logging is configured. 4. Confirm that sensitive data (such as Secret contents) is not logged at the Request or RequestResponse level. 5. Document compliance findings and any necessary modifications to the audit policy.

title: Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the kubelet service file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The kubelet service file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the kubelet service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Automated AAC auditing has been modified to allow CIS-CAT to input a variable for the <PATH>/<FILENAME> of the kubelet service config file. Please set $kubelet_service_config=<PATH> based on the file location on your system for example: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf To perform the audit manually: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/kubeadm.conf Default Value: By default, the kubelet service file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual audit due to the nature of file permissions being an OS-level property that is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps necessitate direct access to the worker nodes to verify and potentially remediate the kubelet service file permissions, which cannot be automated.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node. 2. Identify the kubelet service configuration file location (commonly `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` or similar). 3. Run the following command to check file permissions: ``` stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf ``` (Replace the path with the actual location if different.) 4. Verify that the output is 600 or more restrictive (e.g., 600, 400, 440). 5. Confirm that only the file owner (typically root) has read and write permissions, and no other users have access. 6. If the permissions are more permissive than 600 (e.g., 640, 644, 660), remediate by running: ``` chmod 600 /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf ``` 7. Repeat for all worker nodes in the cluster.

References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in

title: Ensure that the --anonymous-auth argument is set to false
description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
rationale: When enabled, requests that are not rejected by other configured authentication methods are treated as anonymous requests. These requests are then served by the Kubelet server. You should rely on authentication to authorize access and disallow anonymous requests. Impact: Anonymous requests will be rejected.
audit: If using a Kubelet configuration file, check that there is an entry for authentication: anonymous: enabled set to false. Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --anonymous-auth argument is set to false. This executable argument may be omitted, provided there is a corresponding entry set to false in the Kubelet config file.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authentication: anonymous: enabled to false. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. --anonymous-auth=false Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, anonymous access is enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication
impact: Anonymous requests will be rejected.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated, leveraging Kubernetes API and CLI commands to check the `--anonymous-auth` flag and Kubelet configuration. The automation details provided are technically sound and feasible within the Kubernetes environment, allowing for efficient compliance checks across nodes without manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Kubelet - API/CLI Method: kubectl get nodes, kubectl -n kube-system get pod -o yaml, privileged DaemonSet to access /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml - Properties to Check: - Command line argument `--anonymous-auth` - KubeletConfiguration file entry `authentication.anonymous.enabled` - Pass Criteria: - The Kubelet on every node must have `--anonymous-auth=false` OR `authentication.anonymous.enabled: false` in the Kubelet config file. - Fail Criteria: - Any node where `--anonymous-auth` is not set to false AND `authentication.anonymous.enabled` is not false.

title: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow
description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
rationale: Kubelets, by default, allow all authenticated requests (even anonymous ones) without needing explicit authorization checks from the apiserver. You should restrict this behavior and only allow explicitly authorized requests. Impact: Unauthorized requests will be denied.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --authorization-mode argument is present check that it is not set to AlwaysAllow. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by -- config, and that file sets authorization: mode to something other than AlwaysAllow. It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint on the Kubelet API port (typically 10250/TCP). Accessing these with appropriate credentials will provide details of the Kubelet's configuration.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authorization: mode to Webhook. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS variable. --authorization-mode=Webhook Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --authorization-mode argument is set to AlwaysAllow. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication
impact: Unauthorized requests will be denied.
justification: The audit process requires checking the Kubelet configuration directly on each node, which involves accessing the Kubelet's command-line arguments or configuration files. While the first AI opinion suggests an automated approach, the second AI review highlights the complexities involved in accessing the Kubelet's `/configz` endpoint, which necessitates manual setup that cannot be fully automated. Therefore, a manual approach is more appropriate given the need for direct inspection of node configurations.
manual_summary: 1. On each node, run the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to check the running Kubelet process and its arguments. 2. Verify if the `--authorization-mode` argument is present. If it is, ensure that it is not set to `AlwaysAllow`. 3. If the `--authorization-mode` argument is not present, check for a Kubelet configuration file specified by the `--config` flag. Locate this file and open it to verify that it sets `authorization: mode` to a value other than `AlwaysAllow`. 4. Optionally, access the Kubelet's `/configz` endpoint at `https://<node-ip>:10250/configz` using appropriate credentials to review the running configuration. 5. If any Kubelet instance is found with `authorization.mode` set to `AlwaysAllow`, remediate by editing the Kubelet configuration file or service file to set `authorization: mode` to `Webhook`, and restart the Kubelet service using: - `systemctl daemon-reload` - `systemctl restart kubelet.service`

title: Ensure that the --client-ca-file argument is set as appropriate
description: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
rationale: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --client-ca-file argument exists and is set to the location of the client certificate authority file. If the --client-ca-file argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, and that the file sets authentication: x509: clientCAFile to the location of the client certificate authority file.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authentication: x509: clientCAFile to the location of the client CA file. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS variable. --client-ca-file=<path/to/client-ca-file> Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --client-ca-file argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet- authentication-authorization/
impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
justification: The audit requires checking the `--client-ca-file` argument and potentially the kubelet configuration file, which are not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. Both AI opinions agree on the necessity of a manual approach due to the nature of the checks being process-level configurations that require direct OS-level access.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each worker node in the Kubernetes cluster. 2. Run the following command to inspect the kubelet process arguments: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` 3. Check if the `--client-ca-file` argument is present and points to a valid client CA file path. 4. If the `--client-ca-file` argument is not present, check if the kubelet is started with a `--config` argument pointing to a kubelet configuration file. 5. If a kubelet config file is used, open the file (commonly `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` or similar) and verify that under the `authentication` section, the `x509` subsection has the `clientCAFile` property set to the correct CA file path: ```yaml authentication: x509: clientCAFile: /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt ``` 6. Confirm that the specified CA file exists on the node and is the intended client CA. 7. Repeat the above steps for each worker node in the cluster.

title: Verify that if defined, readOnlyPort is set to 0
description: Disable the read-only port.
rationale: The Kubelet process provides a read-only API in addition to the main Kubelet API. Unauthenticated access is provided to this read-only API which could possibly retrieve potentially sensitive information about the cluster. Impact: Removal of the read-only port will require that any service which made use of it will need to be re-configured to use the main Kubelet API.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --read-only-port argument exists and is set to 0. If the --read-only-port argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config. Check that if there is a readOnlyPort entry in the file, it is set to 0.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set readOnlyPort to 0. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. --read-only-port=0 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/6cedc0853faa118df0ba3d41b48b 993422ad3df6/staging/src/k8s.io/kubelet/config/v1beta1/types.go#L142 Additional Information: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet/
impact: Removal of the read-only port will require that any service which made use of it will need to be re-configured to use the main Kubelet API.
justification: The audit steps require access to node-level configurations and process arguments that are not exposed through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions acknowledge that while automation can assist in checking the Kubelet configuration, it ultimately requires manual inspection or privileged access to the nodes, which makes a purely automated approach infeasible.
manual_summary: 1. **Access Each Node:** - SSH into each node in the Kubernetes cluster. 2. **Check Kubelet Process:** - Run the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - Look for the `--read-only-port` argument in the output. If it exists, ensure it is set to `0`. 3. **Check Kubelet Configuration File:** - If the `--read-only-port` argument is not present, check if a Kubelet configuration file is specified by the `--config` flag. - Run: `cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep readOnlyPort` - Verify that the `readOnlyPort` entry is either absent or explicitly set to `0`. 4. **Remediation Steps:** - If the `readOnlyPort` is set to a value other than `0`, edit the Kubelet configuration file to set `readOnlyPort` to `0`. - If using command-line arguments, edit the kubelet service file located at `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf` and set the parameter `--read-only-port=0` in the `KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS` variable. - Restart the kubelet service with the following commands: ``` systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service ``` 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Repeat the checks to ensure that the `readOnlyPort` is now set to `0` or not defined.

title: Ensure that the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument is not set to 0
description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
rationale: Setting idle timeouts ensures that you are protected against Denial-of-Service attacks, inactive connections and running out of ephemeral ports. Note: By default, --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to 4 hours which might be too high for your environment. Setting this as appropriate would additionally ensure that such streaming connections are timed out after serving legitimate use cases. Impact: Long-lived connections could be interrupted.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument is not set to 0. If the argument is not present, and there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, check that it does not set streamingConnectionIdleTimeout to 0.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set streamingConnectionIdleTimeout to a value other than 0. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. --streaming-connection-idle-timeout=5m Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to 4 hours. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/18552
impact: Long-lived connections could be interrupted.
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach should be manual due to the nature of the `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout` argument, which is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or CLI tools. The verification process requires direct access to the node's operating system to inspect the Kubelet process arguments or configuration files, confirming that automation is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each worker node in the Kubernetes cluster. 2. Run the following command to inspect the Kubelet process arguments: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` 3. Check if the `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout` argument is present: - If present, ensure it is NOT set to 0 (e.g., `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout=0` is non-compliant). - If absent, proceed to the next step. 4. Check if the Kubelet is started with a `--config` flag pointing to a configuration file (e.g., `--config=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`). 5. If a config file is used, open the referenced file (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`) and inspect the value of `streamingConnectionIdleTimeout`: - Ensure `streamingConnectionIdleTimeout` is NOT set to 0. 6. The node is compliant if neither the command line argument nor the configuration file specifies a timeout of 0.

title: Ensure that the --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set to true
description: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables.
rationale: Kubelets can automatically manage the required changes to iptables based on how you choose your networking options for the pods. It is recommended to let kubelets manage the changes to iptables. This ensures that the iptables configuration remains in sync with pods networking configuration. Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open. Impact: Kubelet would manage the iptables on the system and keep it in sync. If you are using any other iptables management solution, then there might be some conflicts.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that if the --make-iptables-util-chains argument exists then it is set to true. If the --make-iptables-util-chains argument does not exist, and there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, verify that the file does not set makeIPTablesUtilChains to false.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set makeIPTablesUtilChains: true. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and remove the --make- iptables-util-chains argument from the KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set to true. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/
impact: Kubelet would manage the iptables on the system and keep it in sync. If you are using any other iptables management solution, then there might be some conflicts.
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach must be manual due to the nature of the `--make-iptables-util-chains` argument, which is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. The verification process requires direct access to the host operating system to inspect the Kubelet process arguments and configuration files, making automation infeasible.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node. 2. Run the following command to view the Kubelet process arguments: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` 3. Inspect the process arguments: - If the `--make-iptables-util-chains` flag is present, ensure it is set to `true`. - If the flag is absent, check if the `--config` flag is used to specify a Kubelet config file. 4. If a Kubelet config file is used (commonly located at `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` or similar), open the file and inspect the value of `makeIPTablesUtilChains`: - Ensure `makeIPTablesUtilChains: true` is set, or that the field is not present (default is true). - If `makeIPTablesUtilChains: false` is set, this is non-compliant. 5. Repeat the above steps for all worker nodes. 6. Pass Criteria: All nodes must have `--make-iptables-util-chains=true` set (if present), or `makeIPTablesUtilChains` should not be set to false in the config file.

title: Ensure that the --hostname-override argument is not set
description: Do not override node hostnames.
rationale: Overriding hostnames could potentially break TLS setup between the kubelet and the apiserver. Additionally, with overridden hostnames, it becomes increasingly difficult to associate logs with a particular node and process them for security analytics. Hence, you should setup your kubelet nodes with resolvable FQDNs and avoid overriding the hostnames with IPs. Impact: Some cloud providers may require this flag to ensure that hostname matches names issued by the cloud provider. In these environments, this recommendation should not apply.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that --hostname-override argument does not exist. Note This setting is not configurable via the Kubelet config file.
remediation: Edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf on each worker node and remove the --hostname-override argument from the KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --hostname-override argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/22063
impact: Some cloud providers may require this flag to ensure that hostname matches names issued by the cloud provider. In these environments, this recommendation should not apply.
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit control requires a manual approach due to the necessity of OS-level access to verify the kubelet's command-line arguments, which are not exposed through Kubernetes APIs or accessible via `kubectl`. The audit steps clearly indicate that the check cannot be automated, reinforcing the decision for a manual process.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node. 2. Run the following command to list the kubelet process and its arguments: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` 3. Examine the output for the presence of the `--hostname-override` argument. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** The `--hostname-override` argument should NOT be present in the kubelet process command line. 5. If the argument is present, edit the kubelet service file (commonly `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf`) to remove the `--hostname-override` flag, then reload and restart the kubelet service: ``` systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service ``` 6. Repeat this process for every worker node in the cluster to ensure compliance.

title: Ensure that the eventRecordQPS argument is set to a level which ensures appropriate event capture
description: Security relevant information should be captured. The eventRecordQPS on the Kubelet configuration can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered and sets the maximum event creations per second. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
rationale: It is important to capture all events and not restrict event creation. Events are an important source of security information and analytics that ensure that your environment is consistently monitored using the event data. Impact: Setting this parameter to 0 could result in a denial of service condition due to excessive events being created. The cluster's event processing and storage systems should be scaled to handle expected event loads.
audit: Run the following command on each node: sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf or If using command line arguments, kubelet service file is located /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet- args.conf Review the value set for the argument and determine whether this has been set to an appropriate level for the cluster. If the argument does not exist, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by -- config and review the value in this location. If using command line arguments
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set eventRecordQPS: to an appropriate level. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, eventRecordQPS argument is set to 5. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/apis/kubeletco nfig/v1beta1/types.go
impact: Setting this parameter to 0 could result in a denial of service condition due to excessive events being created. The cluster's event processing and storage systems should be scaled to handle expected event loads.
justification: The audit control for `eventRecordQPS` requires direct access to the kubelet configuration files on each node, which are not exposed through Kubernetes APIs or accessible via kubectl commands. Both AI opinions correctly identify that this check necessitates host-level access, making a manual approach the only feasible option.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node. 2. Determine how the kubelet is configured: - For command-line arguments, execute: ``` sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf ``` or ``` sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf ``` - If a kubelet config file is used, find the config file path (look for the `--config` flag in the kubelet service definition) and run: ``` sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" <kubelet-config-file-path> ``` 3. Review the value of `eventRecordQPS`: - Ensure it is set to a non-zero, appropriate value (default is 5). - If the argument does not exist or is set to an inappropriate value, proceed to remediation. 4. Remediation steps: - If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set `eventRecordQPS` to an appropriate level. - If using command-line arguments, edit the kubelet service file `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` on each worker node to include the desired `eventRecordQPS` value in the `KUBELET_ARGS` variable. - After making changes, run the following commands to apply the changes: ``` sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl restart kubelet.service ```

title: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
description: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets.
rationale: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks.
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments exist and they are set as appropriate. If these arguments are not present, check that there is a Kubelet config specified by -- config and that it contains appropriate settings for tlsCertFile and tlsPrivateKeyFile.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set tlsCertFile to the location of the certificate file to use to identify this Kubelet, and tlsPrivateKeyFile to the location of the corresponding private key file. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameters in KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. --tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file> --tls-private-key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file> Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
justification: The verification of the `--tls-cert-file` and `--tls-private-key-file` arguments requires direct access to the underlying host operating system, as these configurations are not exposed through Kubernetes APIs or accessible via `kubectl`. Both AI opinions correctly identify that this task necessitates manual inspection of the kubelet process and configuration files on each node, making an automated approach infeasible.
manual_summary: 1. On each worker node, access the host OS via SSH or console. 2. Run the following command to inspect the kubelet process: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` 3. Check if the kubelet process includes the flags: - `--tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file>` - `--tls-private-key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file>` 4. If these flags are not present, check if the kubelet is started with a `--config` flag pointing to a configuration file (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`). 5. If a config file is used, open it and verify the presence and correctness of the following fields: - `tlsCertFile: <path/to/tls-certificate-file>` - `tlsPrivateKeyFile: <path/to/tls-key-file>` 6. Confirm that the file paths specified exist on the node and are valid certificate and private key files. 7. Repeat these steps for every worker node in the cluster. 8. Compliance is achieved if all kubelets have the appropriate TLS settings configured.

title: Ensure that the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to false
description: Enable kubelet client certificate rotation.
rationale: The --rotate-certificates setting causes the kubelet to rotate its client certificates by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there is no downtime due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Note: This feature also require the RotateKubeletClientCertificate feature gate to be enabled (which is the default since Kubernetes v1.7) Impact: None
audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is not present, or is set to true. If the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is not present, verify that if there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, that file does not contain RotateKubeletServerCertificate: false.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to add the line rotateCertificates: true or remove it altogether to use the default value. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and remove --rotate- certificates=false argument from the KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable or set - -rotate-certificates=true . Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, kubelet client certificate rotation is enabled. References: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/41912 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet-tls- bootstrapping/#kubelet-configuration 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/imported/release/notes/ 4. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/feature-gates/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach for checking the `--rotate-certificates` argument must be manual. This is due to the fact that this configuration is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`, requiring direct access to the node to inspect the kubelet process or configuration file. Therefore, a manual approach is necessary to ensure compliance.
manual_summary: 1. **Log in to each worker node** in the Kubernetes cluster with appropriate privileges. 2. **Check the kubelet process arguments:** - Run: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - Review the output for the presence of the `--rotate-certificates` flag. - Ensure that `--rotate-certificates=false` is NOT present. If the flag is present, it should be set to `true`. 3. **If a kubelet config file is used** (commonly referenced by the `--config` flag in the process arguments): - Locate the kubelet config file (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` or as specified by `--config`). - Open the file and check for the `rotateCertificates` field. - Ensure that `rotateCertificates: false` is NOT present. If the field exists, it should be set to `true` or omitted (default is `true`). 4. **Expected secure configuration:** - The kubelet is either running with `--rotate-certificates=true` or the `rotateCertificates` field is set to `true` or omitted in the config file.

title: Verify that the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is set to true
description: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation.
rationale: RotateKubeletServerCertificate causes the kubelet to both request a serving certificate after bootstrapping its client credentials and rotate the certificate as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there are no downtimes due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Impact: None
audit: Ignore this check if serverTLSBootstrap is true in the kubelet config file or if the --rotate- server-certificates parameter is set on kubelet Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument exists and is set to true.
remediation: Edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. --feature-gates=RotateKubeletServerCertificate=true Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, kubelet server certificate rotation is enabled. References: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/45059 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-tls-bootstrapping/#kubelet-configuration
impact: None
justification: The audit control requires inspection of node-level configurations or process arguments, which cannot be accessed through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree on the necessity of a manual approach due to the lack of direct API exposure for the RotateKubeletServerCertificate setting. Therefore, manual verification is the only feasible method.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each worker node in the Kubernetes cluster. 2. Run the following command to check the kubelet process arguments: ``` ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` 3. Inspect the output for the presence of the flag: ``` --feature-gates=RotateKubeletServerCertificate=true ``` OR check for the flag: ``` --rotate-server-certificates=true ``` 4. Alternatively, check the kubelet configuration file (commonly at /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml or /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf) for: ``` serverTLSBootstrap: true ``` 5. Confirm that either the feature gate or the config file enables kubelet server certificate rotation. 6. The node is compliant if at least one of the above settings is present and set to true.

title: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
description: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
rationale: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided. Impact: Kubelet clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the Kubelet API.
audit: The set of cryptographic ciphers currently considered secure is the following: • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --tls-cipher-suites argument is present, ensure it only contains values included in this set. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, and that file sets tlsCipherSuites: to only include values from this set.
remediation: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set tlsCipherSuites: to TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM _SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_ 256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WI TH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES _128_GCM_SHA256 or to a subset of these values. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the --tls-cipher- suites parameter as follows, or to a subset of these values. --tls-cipher- suites=TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM _SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM _SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM _SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default the Kubernetes API server supports a wide range of TLS ciphers Additional Information: The list chosen above should be fine for modern clients. It's essentially the list from the Mozilla "Modern cipher" option with the ciphersuites supporting CBC mode removed, as CBC has traditionally had a lot of issues
impact: Kubelet clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the Kubelet API.
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach should be manual due to the nature of the Kubelet's TLS cipher suite configuration, which is not exposed through the Kubernetes API or accessible via `kubectl`. The verification process requires direct access to the node's operating system to inspect process arguments or configuration files, making automation infeasible.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into each Kubernetes worker node.** 2. **Check the running Kubelet process arguments:** - Run: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - Look for the presence of the `--tls-cipher-suites` argument. - If present, ensure it only contains the following values (or a subset): - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 3. **If the `--tls-cipher-suites` argument is not present:** - Check if the Kubelet is started with a `--config` flag pointing to a configuration file. - Open the referenced Kubelet config file (commonly `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` or similar) and verify the `tlsCipherSuites` field. 4. **Ensure compliance:** - If the cipher suites do not match the approved list, update the Kubelet configuration accordingly and restart the Kubelet service.

title: Ensure that a limit is set on pod PIDs
description: Ensure that the Kubelet sets limits on the number of PIDs that can be created by pods running on the node.
rationale: By default pods running in a cluster can consume any number of PIDs, potentially exhausting the resources available on the node. Setting an appropriate limit reduces the risk of a denial of service attack on cluster nodes. Impact: Setting this value will restrict the number of processes per pod. If this limit is lower than the number of PIDs required by a pod it will not operate.
audit: Review the Kubelet's start-up parameters for the value of --pod-max-pids, and check the Kubelet configuration file for the PodPidsLimit . If neither of these values is set, then there is no limit in place.
remediation: Decide on an appropriate level for this parameter and set it, either via the --pod-max- pids command line parameter or the PodPidsLimit configuration file setting. Default Value: By default the number of PIDs is not limited. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/pid-limiting/#pod-pid-limits
impact: Setting this value will restrict the number of processes per pod. If this limit is lower than the number of PIDs required by a pod it will not operate.
justification: The assessment of the compliance control indicates that the configuration can be efficiently checked across multiple nodes using automation. Both AI opinions support the automated approach, highlighting the need to verify the Kubelet's configuration for PID limits, which is not exposed via Kubernetes APIs but can be accessed through SSH or DaemonSet. The proposed program name is appropriate and follows the naming conventions, ensuring clarity in its purpose.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Kubelet - API/CLI Method: SSH or DaemonSet to access Kubelet configuration - Properties to Check: `--pod-max-pids` command-line flag or `podPidsLimit` in the Kubelet configuration file - Pass Criteria: At least one of `podPidsLimit` (in config file) or `--pod-max-pids` (in process args) is set to a positive integer on every worker node. - Fail Criteria: Neither is set, or value is zero/unlimited, on any node.

title: Ensure that the --seccomp-default parameter is set to true
description: Ensure that the Kubelet enforces the use of the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile
rationale: By default, Kubernetes disables the seccomp profile which ships with most container runtimes. Setting this parameter will ensure workloads running on the node are protected by the runtime's seccomp profile. Impact: Setting this will remove some rights from pods running on the node.
audit: Review the Kubelet's start-up parameters for the value of --seccomp-default, and check the Kubelet configuration file for the seccompDefault . If neither of these values is set, then the seccomp profile is not in use.
remediation: Set the parameter, either via the --seccomp-default command line parameter or the seccompDefault configuration file setting. Default Value: By default the seccomp profile is not enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/security/seccomp/#enable-the-use-of- runtimedefault-as-the-default-seccomp-profile-for-all-workloads
impact: Setting this will remove some rights from pods running on the node.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively automated by querying the Kubelet configuration using Kubernetes-native methods such as DaemonSets and Kubernetes APIs. Both AI opinions support automation, with the first opinion providing a comprehensive automated approach, while the second opinion acknowledges the potential for improvement but still validates the automated classification. The reliance on SSH and manual inspection is not necessary, as the required configurations can be accessed programmatically.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Kubelet - API/CLI Method: Use a DaemonSet to run an inspection pod on each node to check the Kubelet configuration file and process arguments. - Properties to Check: - Presence of `--seccomp-default=true` in the Kubelet process arguments. - Presence of `seccompDefault: true` in the Kubelet configuration file (typically located at `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`). - Pass Criteria: At least one of the following must be true for every node: - The Kubelet is started with `--seccomp-default=true` - The Kubelet config file contains `seccompDefault: true` - Fail Criteria: If neither the flag nor the config file setting is present or set to `false` on any node.

title: Ensure that the --IPAddressDeny is set to any
description: Ensuring that --IPAddressDeny is set to "Any" will facilitate allowlisting of only IP addresses that are explicitly set with the --IPAddressAllow parameter which will block unspecified IP addresses from communicating with the kubelet component.
rationale: By default, Kubernetes allows any IP address to communicate with the kubelet component IP restrictions and IP whitelisting are security best practices and reduce the attack surface of the kubelet . Impact: Configuring the setting IPAddressDeny=any will deny service to any IP address not specified in the complimentary setting IPAddressDeny=any configuration parameter. Applying IPAddressDeny=any alone will completely disable communication with the component.
audit: Review the Kubelet's start-up parameters for the value of --IPAddressDeny, and check the Kubelet configuration file for IPAddressDeny=any. If this entry is present it should be accompanied by IPAddressAllow={{ kubelet_secure_addresses }} to allow the control plane to communicate with the component.
remediation: IPAddressDeny=any IPAddressAllow={{ kubelet_secure_addresses }} *Note kubelet_secure_addresses: "localhost link-local {{ kube_pods_subnets | regex_replace(',', ' ') }} {{ kube_node_addresses }} {{ loadbalancer_apiserver.address | default('')" Default Value: By default IPAddressDeny is not enabled. References: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kubespray/pull/9194/files 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/
impact: Configuring the setting IPAddressDeny=any will deny service to any IP address not specified in the complimentary setting IPAddressDeny=any configuration parameter. Applying IPAddressDeny=any alone will completely disable communication with the component.
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach must be manual due to the nature of the kubelet's configuration settings, which are not accessible through the Kubernetes API or kubectl commands. The required parameters, `--IPAddressDeny` and `--IPAddressAllow`, can only be verified through direct access to the node's file system or process command-line arguments, necessitating SSH access to the worker nodes.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the worker node(s):** SSH into each Kubernetes worker node running the kubelet. 2. **Locate the kubelet configuration:** - Check the kubelet’s systemd unit file, typically found at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` or `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf`, for command-line arguments. - Alternatively, check the kubelet configuration file, often located at `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` or as specified by the `--config` flag. 3. **Verify the --IPAddressDeny setting:** - In the command-line arguments or configuration file, confirm that `--IPAddressDeny=any` is present. 4. **Verify the --IPAddressAllow setting:** - Ensure that `--IPAddressAllow` is set to the required allowlist (e.g., `localhost`, `link-local`, and any other specified secure addresses).

title: Ensure that the kube-proxy metrics service is bound to localhost
description: Do not bind the kube-proxy metrics port to non-loopback addresses.
rationale: kube-proxy has two APIs which provided access to information about the service and can be bound to network ports. The metrics API service includes endpoints (/metrics and /configz) which disclose information about the configuration and operation of kube-proxy. These endpoints should not be exposed to untrusted networks as they do not support encryption or authentication to restrict access to the data they provide. Impact: 3rd party services which try to access metrics or configuration information related to kube-proxy will require access to the localhost interface of the node.
audit: review the start-up flags provided to kube proxy Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep -i kube-proxy Ensure that the --metrics-bind-address parameter is not set to a value other than 127.0.0.1. From the output of this command gather the location specified in the -- config parameter. Review any file stored at that location and ensure that it does not specify a value other than 127.0.0.1 for metricsBindAddress.
remediation: Modify or remove any values which bind the metrics service to a non-localhost address Default Value: The default value is 127.0.0.1:10249 References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube-proxy/
impact: 3rd party services which try to access metrics or configuration information related to kube-proxy will require access to the localhost interface of the node.
justification: The control requires verification of the kube-proxy process startup flags and configuration files on each node, which are not accessible through Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. Both AI opinions agree that this check necessitates host-level access to inspect process arguments and configuration files, confirming that a manual approach is necessary.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into each worker node** where kube-proxy is running. 2. **Check the kube-proxy process arguments** by running: ``` ps -ef | grep -i kube-proxy ``` - Look for the `--metrics-bind-address` flag. - Ensure it is set to `127.0.0.1` or not set (default is `127.0.0.1:10249`). 3. **If kube-proxy uses a config file** (specified with `--config`), locate the file path from the process arguments. - Open the config file and check the `metricsBindAddress` field. - Ensure it is set to `127.0.0.1` or not set. 4. **Expected secure configuration:** - `--metrics-bind-address=127.0.0.1` or not set (default is secure). - In the config file: `metricsBindAddress: 127.0.0.1` 5. **Confirm compliance:** If all kube-proxy instances are bound to `127.0.0.1` for metrics, the node is compliant. If any instance binds to a non-loopback address, it is non-compliant.

title: Ensure that the cluster-admin role is only used where required
description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
rationale: Kubernetes provides a set of default roles where RBAC is used. Some of these roles such as cluster-admin provide wide-ranging privileges which should only be applied where absolutely necessary.
