automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get clusterrole,role --all-namespaces -o json` and `kubectl get clusterrolebinding,rolebinding --all-namespaces -o yaml` - Properties to Check: `rules` field for `resources: ["serviceaccounts/token"]` with `verbs` including `create` - Pass Criteria: No non-system users, groups, or service accounts have `create` permissions on the `serviceaccounts/token` subresource. - Fail Criteria: Any non-system user, group, or service account is found with `create` permissions on the `serviceaccounts/token` subresource.

title: Ensure that the cluster has at least one active policy control mechanism in place
description: Every Kubernetes cluster should have at least one policy control mechanism in place to enforce the other requirements in this section. This could be the in-built Pod Security Admission controller, or a third party policy control system.
rationale: Without an active policy control mechanism, it is not possible to limit the use of containers with access to underlying cluster nodes, via mechanisms like privileged containers, or the use of hostPath volume mounts. Impact: Where policy control systems are in place, there is a risk that workloads required for the operation of the cluster may be stopped from running. Care is required when implementing admission control policies to ensure that this does not occur.
audit: Pod Security Admission is enabled by default on all clusters using Kubernetes 1.23 or higher. To assess what controls, if any, are in place using this mechanism, review the namespaces in the cluster to see if the required labels have been applied kubectl get namespaces -o yaml To confirm if any external policy control system is in use.
remediation: Ensure that either Pod Security Admission or an external policy control system is in place for every namespace which contains user workloads. Default Value: By default, Pod Security Standards define three different policies available to apply. Privileged, Baseline or Restricted. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-admission 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Where policy control systems are in place, there is a risk that workloads required for the operation of the cluster may be stopped from running. Care is required when implementing admission control policies to ensure that this does not occur.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach, highlighting the feasibility of using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands to check for policy control mechanisms. The audit steps outlined in the compliance control data can be effectively automated using `kubectl` to enumerate namespaces and verify the presence of Pod Security Admission labels and third-party policy controllers. This aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes, making automation the most efficient and reliable method.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces -o yaml` - Properties to Check: Presence of labels `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn`, and validation of their values against Pod Security Standards (`privileged`, `baseline`, `restricted`). - Pass Criteria: At least one namespace containing user workloads has valid Pod Security Admission labels or an external policy control mechanism is detected (e.g., relevant CRDs for OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno). - Fail Criteria: No namespaces with valid Pod Security Admission labels and no external policy control mechanisms detected.

title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true.
rationale: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. Privleged pods disable most security mechanisms and should be disallowed. Impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true, spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of privileged containers. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add security context which has user workloads to restrict the admission of privileged containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of privileged containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true, spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
justification: The control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands to check for privileged containers across all namespaces. Both AI opinions support the automated approach, and the commands provided are technically sound for identifying privileged containers and ensuring compliance with the specified policies. The automation can cover the necessary checks without requiring extensive manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged`, `spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged`, `spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged` - Pass Criteria: All values of `privileged` should be `false` for all containers. - Fail Criteria: Any container with `privileged: true` indicates non-compliance.

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host process ID namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's PID namespace can inspect processes running outside the container. If the container also has access to ptrace capabilities this can be used to escalate privileges outside of the container. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host PID namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostPID, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.hostPID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostPID containers. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostPID containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPID containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostPID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation. The first opinion provides a clear automated approach with specific commands and checks, while the second opinion acknowledges the automation potential but suggests improvements. Given the capabilities of Kubernetes APIs to enumerate namespaces and check policies, an automated approach is both practical and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get namespaces` - `kubectl get namespace <ns> -o json` - `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - `kubectl get psp -o yaml` - Properties to Check: - For namespaces: `metadata.labels.pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` (should be `restricted` or equivalent) - For Pod Security Policies: `spec.hostPID` (should be `false` or not allowed) - For pods: `spec.hostPID` (should not be `true` unless explicitly allowed) - Pass Criteria: - No pods in any namespace are running with `spec.hostPID: true` unless explicitly allowed by a narrowly scoped policy. - All namespaces with user workloads have Pod Security Admission enforced at the `restricted` level or equivalent, which denies `hostPID: true`. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacks a policy that restricts `hostPID: true`, or any pod is found running with `spec.hostPID: true` outside of an explicitly allowed and controlled context.

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host IPC namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostIPC flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's IPC namespace can use IPC to interact with processes outside the container. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host IPC namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostIPC, this should be definited in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.hostIPC: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostIPC containers. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostIPC containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostIPC containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostIPC: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The majority of the compliance checks can be effectively performed using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as outlined in both AI opinions. While the second opinion highlights the need for manual verification of documented exceptions, the core audit steps can still be automated. The proposed program name is appropriate and aligns with the compliance control's intent.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: Namespace labels for Pod Security Admission (e.g., `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`), Pod specifications for `spec.hostIPC` - Pass Criteria: - All namespaces with user workloads have the `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` label set to `baseline` or `restricted`. - No pods are running with `spec.hostIPC: true` unless in namespaces with explicit, documented exceptions. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacks the required Pod Security Admission labels. - Any pod is found running with `spec.hostIPC: true` in non-exempt namespaces.

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host network namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostNetwork flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's network namespace could access the local loopback device, and could access network traffic to and from other pods. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host network namespace. If you need to run containers which require access to the host's network namesapces, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.hostNetwork: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostNetwork containers. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostNetwork containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostNetwork containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostNetwork: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The majority of the audit steps can be effectively automated using Kubernetes API calls and `kubectl` commands, as outlined in the first AI opinion. While the second AI opinion raised concerns about the need for manual inspection in complex environments, the fundamental checks for compliance regarding the `hostNetwork` flag can be reliably performed through automation. Therefore, an automated approach is appropriate.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get ns --show-labels` - `kubectl get ns <namespace> -o json | jq '.metadata.labels'` - `kubectl get psp -o yaml` - `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.hostNetwork == true)'` - Properties to Check: - Pod Security Admission labels: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version` - Pod Security Policies (if applicable) to ensure `hostNetwork: true` is disallowed. - Pass Criteria: - All namespaces should have the appropriate Pod Security Admission labels enforcing at least the `baseline` or `restricted` level. - No existing pods should be using `hostNetwork: true` unless explicitly allowed by tightly scoped policies. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacking the required Pod Security Admission labels or having pods with `hostNetwork: true` without proper policy enforcement.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with allowPrivilegeEscalation
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true. Allowing this right can lead to a process running a container getting more rights than it started with. It's important to note that these rights are still constrained by the overall container sandbox, and this setting does not relate to the use of privileged containers.
rationale: A container running with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true may have processes that can gain more privileges than their parent. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to allow privilege escalation. The option exists (and is defaulted to true) to permit setuid binaries to run. If you have need to run containers which use setuid binaries or require privilege escalation, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which allow privilege escalation. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of conatiners with .spec.allowPrivilegeEscalationset to true. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on contained process ability to escalate privileges, within the context of the container. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, as both AI opinions support an automated approach. The first opinion provides a detailed automation strategy, while the second opinion acknowledges the feasibility of automation, despite suggesting some manual verification for complex environments. Given the capabilities of Kubernetes to check for Pod Security Admission configurations and the ability to enumerate policies and pods, an automated approach is both practical and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces, PodSecurityPolicies - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get ns --show-labels` to check namespace labels for Pod Security Admission. - `kubectl get psp -o yaml` to enumerate PodSecurityPolicies. - `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation}'` to check running pods. - Properties to Check: - Namespace labels for `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` set to `restricted` or `baseline`. - PodSecurityPolicies ensuring `allowPrivilegeEscalation` is not set to true. - Pods' `securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation` field. - Pass Criteria: - All namespaces have the appropriate Pod Security Admission labels. - No PodSecurityPolicies allow `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`. - No running pods have `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacking the required Pod Security Admission label. - Any PodSecurityPolicy allowing `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`. - Any running pod with `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`.

title: Minimize the admission of root containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run as the root user.
rationale: Containers may run as any Linux user. Containers which run as the root user, whilst constrained by Container Runtime security features still have a escalated likelihood of container breakout. Ideally, all containers should run as a defined non-UID 0 user. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit root containers. If you need to run root containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy restricts the use of root containers by setting MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Create a policy for each namespace in the cluster, ensuring that either MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0, is set. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of root containers and if a User is not specified in the image, the container will run as root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach, and the audit steps can be effectively executed using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. The automation details provided are comprehensive and technically sound, allowing for a thorough check of compliance regarding root containers in Kubernetes. The use of `kubectl` commands and the ability to query policies and pod specifications make this approach feasible and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get ns`, `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: - For Pod Security Admission: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` label values - For PodSecurityPolicy: `runAsUser.rule` values - For Pod specifications: `securityContext.runAsNonRoot` and `securityContext.runAsUser` - Pass Criteria: - Every namespace has an enforced policy that prohibits containers from running as root (UID 0). - No pods are running with containers where `runAsUser: 0` or `runAsNonRoot: false` or unset. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacks such a policy, or any pod/container is found running as root.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability
description: Do not generally permit containers with the potentially dangerous NET_RAW capability.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. By default this can include potentially dangerous capabilities. With Docker as the container runtime the NET_RAW capability is enabled which may be misused by malicious containers. Ideally, all containers should drop this capability. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers with the NET_RAW capability. If you need to run containers with this capability, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy disallows the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with the NET_RAW capability. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
justification: The audit control can be effectively assessed using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of an automated approach. The first opinion provides a comprehensive set of commands and checks that can be executed to validate compliance with the control regarding the NET_RAW capability. The second opinion supports this by validating the automation's appropriateness, indicating that while manual review may be beneficial in complex environments, the primary assessment can be automated.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Policies, Namespace labels, Admission Controllers (Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get psp`, `kubectl get ns --show-labels`, `kubectl get clusterpolicy,policy -A -o yaml`, `kubectl get constrainttemplates,constraints -A -o yaml` - Properties to Check: - For PSP: `.spec.requiredDropCapabilities` should include `NET_RAW` or `.spec.allowedCapabilities` should NOT include `NET_RAW`. - For PSA: Namespaces should have `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted`. - For Kyverno/Gatekeeper: Policies should explicitly deny or drop the `NET_RAW` capability. - Pass Criteria: At least one policy in each namespace disallowing the `NET_RAW` capability. - Fail Criteria: Absence of policies that restrict the `NET_RAW` capability in any namespace with user workloads.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with added capabilities
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities assigned beyond the default set.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities outside this set can be added to containers which could expose them to risks of container breakout attacks. There should be at least one policy defined which prevents containers with capabilities beyond the default set from launching. If you need to run containers with additional capabilities, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities outwith the default set will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that policies are present which prevent allowedCapabilities to be set to anything other than an empty array. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Ensure that allowedCapabilities is not present in policies for the cluster unless it is set to an empty array. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on adding capabilities to containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities outwith the default set will not be permitted.
justification: The audit steps outlined in the compliance control can be effectively executed using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, making automation feasible. Both AI opinions support an automated approach, with the first opinion providing a comprehensive method for checking compliance, while the second opinion acknowledges the potential for manual inspection but ultimately agrees that the checks can be automated. Given the technical capabilities of Kubernetes, this approach is efficient and scalable.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Policies (PSPs) and Pod Security Admission (PSA) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get psp -o yaml`, `kubectl get namespaces -L pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o yaml`, `kubectl get deployments --all-namespaces -o yaml` - Properties to Check: `allowedCapabilities` field in PSPs, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` label in Namespaces, `securityContext.capabilities.add` field in Pods/Deployments - Pass Criteria: - All PSPs have `allowedCapabilities` unset or set to `[]`. - All Namespaces enforce the `restricted` Pod Security Standard via PSA labels. - No Pods/Deployments have containers with `securityContext.capabilities.add` set to non-empty values unless explicitly allowed by policy. - Fail Criteria: - Any PSP allows additional capabilities (`allowedCapabilities` contains values). - Any Namespace does not enforce the `restricted` Pod Security Standard.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with capabilities assigned
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities are parts of the rights generally granted on a Linux system to the root user. In many cases applications running in containers do not require any capabilities to operate, so from the perspective of the principal of least privilege use of capabilities should be minimized. Impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy requires that capabilities are dropped by all containers. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Review the use of capabilities in applications running on your cluster. Where a namespace contains applications which do not require any Linux capabilities to operate consider adding a policy which forbids the admission of containers which do not drop all capabilities. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with additional capabilities References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and-unprivileged- linux-containers/
impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
justification: The majority of the compliance checks can be effectively performed using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, as outlined in both AI opinions. While the second opinion suggests that some manual oversight might be beneficial, the core checks can be automated without requiring human judgment, thus supporting an automated approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[*].securityContext.capabilities` - Pass Criteria: At least one policy per namespace enforces dropping all capabilities for all containers; no running Pods have containers with added capabilities unless explicitly allowed by policy. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace lacks a policy requiring capabilities to be dropped; any running Pods have containers with added capabilities.

title: Minimize the admission of Windows HostProcess Containers
description: Do not generally permit Windows containers to be run with the hostProcess flag set to true.
rationale: A Windows container making use of the hostProcess flag can interact with the underlying Windows cluster node. As per the Kubernetes documentation, this provides "privileged access" to the Windows node. Where Windows containers are used inside a Kubernetes cluster, there should be at least one admission control policy which does not permit hostProcess Windows containers. If you need to run Windows containers which require hostProcess, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostProcess containers. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostProcess containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostProcess containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/create-hostprocess-pod/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The audit process can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation. The necessary checks can be performed programmatically to ensure compliance with the control regarding the admission of Windows HostProcess containers. The automation details provided are comprehensive and align with Kubernetes capabilities.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces, Pod Security Policies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces`, `kubectl get psp -o yaml`, `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: - Namespace labels for Pod Security Admission (e.g., `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`) - Pod Security Policies that deny `spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true` - Existing pods with `spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true` - Pass Criteria: - All namespaces have policies that restrict `hostProcess: true` - No existing pods with `hostProcess: true` are found - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacks a policy that restricts `hostProcess: true` - Any existing pods are found with `hostProcess: true`

title: Minimize the admission of HostPath volumes
description: Do not generally admit containers which make use of hostPath volumes.
rationale: A container which mounts a hostPath volume as part of its specification will have access to the filesystem of the underlying cluster node. The use of hostPath volumes may allow containers access to privileged areas of the node filesystem. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to mount hostPath volumes. If you need to run containers which require hostPath volumes, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a spefific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers with hostPath volumes. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPath volumes. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPath volumes. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a spefific policy.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be effectively audited and enforced using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. The automation details provided are technically sound and leverage existing Kubernetes capabilities to check for compliance regarding hostPath volumes. This approach minimizes the risk of human error and ensures consistent enforcement of policies across namespaces.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA), PodSecurityPolicies (PSP), Pods - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get namespaces` - `kubectl get namespace <ns> -o json` - `kubectl get psp -o yaml` - `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: - For PSA: Check `metadata.labels` for `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit`, and `pod-security.kubernetes.io.warn` to ensure they are set to at least `restricted`. - For PSP: Ensure `volumes` does not include `hostPath` or is tightly restricted. - For Pods: Check for any `spec.volumes[*].hostPath` fields. - Pass Criteria: - Every namespace with user workloads has a policy that denies admission of pods with `hostPath` volumes, or restricts it to only explicitly allowed cases. - No pods are admitted with `hostPath` volumes unless explicitly allowed by policy. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace allowing `hostPath` volumes without appropriate restrictions. - Any pods present with `spec.volumes[*].hostPath` fields that are not compliant with the defined policies.

title: Minimize the admission of containers which use HostPorts
description: Do not generally permit containers which require the use of HostPorts.
rationale: Host ports connect containers directly to the host's network. This can bypass controls such as network policy. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers which require the use of HostPorts. If you need to run containers which require HostPorts, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which have hostPort sections. This audit may also require that you review the securityContext in the Pod specification yaml.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPort sections. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of HostPorts. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/ 5.3 Network Policies and CNI
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes API calls and `kubectl` commands, which allows for a systematic and automated check of namespace policies regarding `hostPort` usage. Both AI opinions support automation, with the first opinion providing a comprehensive approach and the second opinion validating the use of Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands, despite minor suggestions for improvement.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission, PodSecurityPolicies, OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno - API/CLI Method: - `kubectl get ns --show-labels` - `kubectl get ns <namespace> -o json | jq '.metadata.labels'` - `kubectl get constraints -A | grep hostPort` - `kubectl get psp -o yaml | grep -A 10 hostPorts` - Properties to Check: - Pod Security Admission labels (should indicate `restricted`) - Presence of policies that disallow `hostPort` - Pass Criteria: - All namespaces have a policy that restricts the use of `hostPort` - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace lacks a policy that restricts `hostPort` or has a `hostPort` defined in its Pod specifications.

title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None
audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
remediation: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: None
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
justification: The control requires verifying the capabilities of the CNI plugin regarding Network Policies, which necessitates consulting external documentation that is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. While some aspects, such as identifying the CNI plugin, can be automated, the critical verification step of confirming support for Ingress and Egress network policies must be done manually. This aligns with both AI opinions, which emphasize the need for human review in this context.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the CNI plugin in use: - Run: `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` - Look for pods with names like calico, weave, flannel, cilium, etc., to determine the CNI plugin deployed. 2. Review the official documentation for the identified CNI plugin: - Confirm that the plugin supports both Ingress and Egress Kubernetes Network Policies. - Example: For Calico, verify support at https://docs.projectcalico.org/security/kubernetes-network-policy. 3. Confirm that the plugin is configured and running in the cluster: - Check that the CNI DaemonSet is healthy and all pods are running. - Run: `kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system` 4. Document your findings: - Note the CNI plugin name, version, and documentation link confirming support for Network Policies. - If the plugin does not support Network Policies, mark as non-compliant.

title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace. Impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces``` Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them. Default Value: By default, network policies are not created. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/networkpolicies/ 2. https://octetz.com/posts/k8s-network-policy-apis 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/declare-network-policy/
impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be fully automated using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. The task of checking for NetworkPolicy objects across all namespaces is straightforward and can be executed programmatically, making automation the most efficient and effective approach.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: NetworkPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace>` - Properties to Check: Presence of at least one NetworkPolicy object in each namespace - Pass Criteria: Every namespace in the cluster contains at least one NetworkPolicy object. - Fail Criteria: One or more namespaces do not have any NetworkPolicy objects defined.

title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
rationale: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs. Impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
audit: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
remediation: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables. Default Value: By default, secrets are not defined References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be effectively audited using automated methods via `kubectl` and appropriate filtering tools like `jq` or `jsonpath`. The task involves querying Kubernetes resources and does not require manual inspection or SSH access, making it suitable for automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Deployments, ReplicaSets, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs - API/CLI Method: kubectl get all -A -o jsonpath - Properties to Check: `.spec.containers[*].env` and `.spec.initContainers[*].env` for `valueFrom.secretKeyRef` - Pass Criteria: No containers or initContainers in any Pod-spec resource use `env.valueFrom.secretKeyRef` - Fail Criteria: Any Pod-spec resource has a container or initContainer with an environment variable sourced from a secret via `secretKeyRef`

title: Consider external secret storage
description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
rationale: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments. Impact: None
audit: Review your secrets management implementation.
remediation: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution. Default Value: By default, no external secret management is configured.
impact: None
justification: The control requires a comprehensive review of secret management implementations, which cannot be fully assessed through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands alone. Both AI opinions agree that while some aspects could be partially automated (like checking for certain CRDs), the core assessment necessitates human review of deployment patterns and documentation, making a manual approach the most appropriate.
manual_summary: 1. Review the cluster documentation and deployment manifests to identify if an external secrets management system (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) is integrated with Kubernetes workloads. 2. Inspect workload manifests (Deployments, StatefulSets, etc.) for the usage of external secrets integrations, such as CSI Secret Store drivers, external-secrets.io CRDs, or sidecar containers that fetch secrets from external providers. 3. Verify that the external secrets management solution requires authentication to access secrets, supports auditing of access, and encrypts secrets both at rest and in transit. 4. Document findings and ensure that any identified external secrets management solutions are compliant with organizational policies regarding secret management.

title: Configure Image Provenance using ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller
description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
rationale: Kubernetes supports plugging in provenance rules to accept or reject the images in your deployments. You could configure such rules to ensure that only approved images are deployed in the cluster. Impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that image provenance is configured as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup image provenance. Default Value: By default, image provenance is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#imagepolicywebhook 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design- proposals/image-provenance.md 3. https://hub.docker.com/r/dnurmi/anchore-toolbox/ 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/22888
impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
justification: The compliance control for configuring image provenance can be effectively automated using Kubernetes CLI commands to verify the configuration of the ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller. Both AI opinions support an automated approach, and the technical capabilities of Kubernetes APIs allow for structured queries to validate the necessary configurations without manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: kube-apiserver - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: - The presence of `ImagePolicyWebhook` in the enabled admission plugins. - The correctness of the `--admission-control-config-file` flag in the kube-apiserver manifest. - Pass Criteria: - The kube-apiserver is running with `ImagePolicyWebhook` enabled. - The `--admission-control-config-file` flag references a valid configuration file. - Fail Criteria: - The `ImagePolicyWebhook` is not listed in the enabled admission plugins. - The `--admission-control-config-file` flag is missing or references an invalid configuration file.

title: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
rationale: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default. You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users. Impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
audit: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them. Default Value: By default, Kubernetes starts with 4 initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with- objects/namespaces/#viewing-namespaces 2. http://blog.kubernetes.io/2016/08/security-best-practices-kubernetes- deployment.html 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-node/589- efficient-node-heartbeats
profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands to enumerate namespaces and verify their existence. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation for the primary checks, and the automation details provided are valid and actionable. While the advanced RBAC check may require additional scripting, the core requirement of confirming non-system namespaces can be fully automated.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces - API/CLI Method: kubectl get namespaces - Properties to Check: Existence of non-system namespaces (i.e., any namespace other than default, kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) - Pass Criteria: At least one non-system namespace exists. - Fail Criteria: Only system namespaces are present; all user workloads are in the "default" namespace.

title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container. Impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault Default Value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ 2. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/
impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated effectively using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools. The task of checking seccomp profiles is straightforward and can be performed programmatically by enumerating Pods and inspecting their security contexts. The automation details provided are accurate and feasible within the capabilities of Kubernetes.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: `.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type` at both Pod and container levels - Pass Criteria: Every Pod (or all containers within the Pod) must have `seccompProfile.type` set to `RuntimeDefault`. - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container missing this setting, or set to `Unconfined` or any value other than `RuntimeDefault`.

title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context. Impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers. Default Value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/security-context/ 2. https://learn.cisecurity.org/benchmarks
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach should be automated, as the task of enumerating Pods and checking their security contexts can be efficiently performed using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. The automation details provided are technically sound and align with the capabilities of Kubernetes, allowing for a comprehensive check of security contexts across all Pods and containers.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: spec.securityContext at both Pod and container levels, specifically looking for fields such as runAsUser, runAsGroup, fsGroup, capabilities, allowPrivilegeEscalation, privileged, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile, etc. - Pass Criteria: Every Pod and every container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) must have a non-null securityContext field defined with at least one security-relevant property set. - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) that lacks a securityContext field or has an empty securityContext object (i.e., no security-relevant properties set).

title: The default namespace should not be used
description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
rationale: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources. Impact: None
audit: Run this command to list objects in default namespace kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default The only entries there should be system managed resources such as the kubernetes service
remediation: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace. Default Value: Unless a namespace is specific on object creation, the default namespace will be used
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be efficiently performed using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands, which are suitable for automation. The audit steps provided in the compliance control data can be executed programmatically, allowing for consistent and repeatable checks of the default namespace usage. The technical accuracy of the commands and the ability to automate the process further support this decision.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaced resources (e.g., Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: Presence of user-created resources in the default namespace - Pass Criteria: Only system-managed resources (e.g., the `kubernetes` service) exist in the `default` namespace; no user/application workloads or custom resources are present. - Fail Criteria: Any user-created resource (e.g., Deployments, Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, etc.) is found in the `default` namespace.

title: Ensure that the API server pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control requires manual verification due to the necessity of accessing the host filesystem to check file permissions, which is not possible through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit steps provided are accurate and necessary for ensuring compliance with the specified file permission requirements.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the control plane node where the kube-apiserver is running. 2. Locate the API server pod specification file, typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. 3. Run the following command to check the file permissions: ``` stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` or ``` ls -l /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 4. Verify that the permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive (e.g., 600, 400, 440, etc.). 5. Confirm compliance if the permissions are 600 or more restrictive; otherwise, remediate by running: ``` chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 6. Document the result for audit purposes.

title: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root.
rationale: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file ownership is set to root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that this control requires a manual approach due to the nature of file ownership being an OS-level property that cannot be accessed or modified through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl. The audit and remediation steps necessitate direct interaction with the host filesystem, which confirms the need for manual verification.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Kubernetes control plane node where the API server runs. 2. Locate the kube-apiserver pod specification file, typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. 3. Run the following command to check the file ownership: ``` stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 4. Verify that the output is: ``` root:root ``` 5. If the ownership is not `root:root`, remediate by running: ``` chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml ``` 6. manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node. 2. Run the following command to check the ownership of the controller-manager.conf file (adjust the path if your configuration uses a different location): ``` stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf ``` 3. Verify that the output is: ``` root:root ``` 4. If the ownership is not root:root, remediate by running: ``` chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf ``` 5. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat command and ensuring the output is root:root.

title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root.
rationale: Kubernetes makes use of a number of certificates as part of its operation. You should set the ownership of the directory containing the PKI information and all files in that directory to maintain their integrity. The directory and files should be owned by root:root. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/ Verify that the ownership of all files and directories in this hierarchy is set to root:root.
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chown -R root:root /etc/kubernetes/pki/ Default Value: By default, the /etc/kubernetes/pki/ directory and all of the files and directories contained within it, are set to be owned by the root user. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: The control requires verification of file and directory ownership on the host filesystem, which is not accessible via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that this check must be performed manually, as it involves direct interaction with the underlying operating system rather than Kubernetes resources.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node. 2. Run the following command to recursively list ownership of all files and directories in the PKI directory: ``` ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/ ``` 3. Review the output and verify that every file and directory is owned by user root and group root (owner: root, group: root). 4. Confirm compliance if all files and directories under /etc/kubernetes/pki/ are owned by root:root. If any file or directory is not, remediate by running: ``` chown -R root:root /etc/kubernetes/pki/ ``` 5. Document the results for audit purposes.

title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI certificate file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
description: Ensure that Kubernetes PKI certificate files have permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
rationale: Kubernetes makes use of a number of certificate files as part of the operation of its components. The permissions on these files should be set to 600 or more restrictive to protect their integrity. Impact: None
audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c '%a' /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive. or ls -l /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt Verify -rw------
remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod -R 600 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt Default Value: By default, the certificates used by Kubernetes are set to have permissions of 644 References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None
justification: The audit control requires verification of file permissions on the Kubernetes PKI certificate files, which cannot be accessed or validated through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that this task necessitates direct access to the host operating system, confirming that a manual approach is the only viable option.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to each Kubernetes control plane node via SSH or console access. 2. Run the following command to list the permissions of all Kubernetes PKI certificate files: ``` ls -l /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt ``` Or, for a more concise output: ``` stat -c '%a %n' /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt ``` 3. Review the output for each certificate file. The permissions should be 600 or more restrictive (i.e., only the owner has read and write permissions; no permissions for group or others). 4. Confirm compliance: All files must show permissions of 600 (-rw-------) or more restrictive (e.g., 400, 440, 444). If any file has permissions less restrictive than 600 (e.g., 644, 664, 666), this is non-compliant. 5. If non-compliant, remediate by running: ``` chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt ``` and re-verify the permissions.

title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI key file permissions are set to 600

For example, chmod -R 600 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key Default Value: By default, the keys used by Kubernetes are set to have permissions of 600 References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: None

manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node. 2. title: Ensure that the --anonymous-auth argument is set to false
description: Disable anonymous requests to the API server.
rationale: When enabled, requests that are not rejected by other configured authentication methods are treated as anonymous requests. These requests are then served by the API server. You should rely on authentication to authorize access and disallow anonymous requests. If you are using RBAC authorization, it is generally considered reasonable to allow anonymous access to the API Server for health checks and discovery purposes, and hence this recommendation is not scored. However, you should consider whether anonymous discovery is an acceptable risk for your purposes. Impact: Anonymous requests will be rejected.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --anonymous-auth argument is set to false. Alternative Audit kubectl get pod -nkube-system -lcomponent=kube-apiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[ ]}{.spec.containers[ ].command} {"\n"}{end}' | grep '--anonymous-auth' | grep -i false If the exit code is '1', then the control isn't present / failed
remediation: Edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- apiserver.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the below parameter. --anonymous-auth=false Default Value: By default, anonymous access is enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/authentication/#anonymous-requests
impact: Anonymous requests will be rejected.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be effectively checked using Kubernetes APIs and the `kubectl` CLI. The audit steps provided in the compliance control data can be automated without requiring manual inspection or SSH access, making it feasible to implement an automated solution. title: Ensure that the --token-auth-file parameter is not set
description: Do not use token based authentication.
rationale: The token-based authentication utilizes static tokens to authenticate requests to the apiserver. The tokens are stored in clear-text in a file on the apiserver, and cannot be revoked or rotated without restarting the apiserver. Hence, do not use static token- based authentication. Impact: You will have to configure and use alternate authentication mechanisms such as certificates. Static token based authentication could not be used.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --token-auth-file argument does not exist. Alternative Audit Method kubectl get pod -nkube-system -lcomponent=kube-apiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[ ]}{.spec.containers[ ].command} {"\n"}{end}' | grep '--token-auth-file' | grep -i false If the exit code is '1', then the control isn't present / failed
remediation: Follow the documentation and configure alternate mechanisms for authentication. Then, edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- apiserver.yaml on the master node and remove the --token-auth-file=<filename> parameter. Default Value: By default, --token-auth-file argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/authentication/#static-token-file 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: You will have to configure and use alternate authentication mechanisms such as certificates. Static token based authentication could not be used.

title: Ensure that the DenyServiceExternalIPs is set
description: This admission controller rejects all net-new usage of the Service field externalIPs.
rationale: Most users do not need the ability to set the externalIPs field for a Service at all, and cluster admins should consider disabling this functionality by enabling the DenyServiceExternalIPs admission controller. Clusters that do need to allow this functionality should consider using some custom policy to manage its usage. Impact: When enabled, users of the cluster may not create new Services which use externalIPs and may not add new values to externalIPs on existing Service objects.

Default Value: By default, --disable-admission-plugins=DenyServiceExternalIP argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/admission-controllers/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
impact: When enabled, users of the cluster may not create new Services which use externalIPs and may not add new values to externalIPs on existing Service objects.

title: Ensure that the --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet- client-key arguments are set as appropriate
description: Enable certificate based kubelet authentication.
rationale: The apiserver, by default, does not authenticate itself to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoints. The requests from the apiserver are treated anonymously. You should set up certificate- based kubelet authentication to ensure that the apiserver authenticates itself to kubelets when submitting requests. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet-client-key arguments exist and they are set as appropriate. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/ 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/master-node- communication/#apiserver---kubelet

title: Ensure that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument is set as appropriate
description: Verify kubelet's certificate before establishing connection.
rationale: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument exists and is set as appropriate. remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup the TLS connection between the apiserver and kubelets. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/ 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/master-node- communication/#apiserver---kubelet
impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.

title: Ensure that the admission control plugin EventRateLimit is set
description: Limit the rate at which the API server accepts requests.
rationale: Using EventRateLimit admission control enforces a limit on the number of events that the API Server will accept in a given time slice. A misbehaving workload could overwhelm and DoS the API Server, making it unavailable. This particularly applies to a multi-tenant cluster, where there might be a small percentage of misbehaving tenants which could have a significant impact on the performance of the cluster overall. Hence, it is recommended to limit the rate of events that the API server will accept. Note: This is an Alpha feature in the Kubernetes 1.15 release. Impact: You need to carefully tune in limits as per your environment.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --enable-admission-plugins argument is set to a value that includes EventRateLimit.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and set the desired limits in a configuration file. Then, edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- apiserver.yaml and set the below parameters. --enable-admission-plugins=...,EventRateLimit,... --admission-control-config-file=<path/to/configuration/file> Default Value: By default, EventRateLimit is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#eventratelimit 3. https://github.com/staebler/community/blob/9873b632f4d99b5d99c38c9b15fe2f8 b93d0a746/contributors/design- proposals/admission_control_event_rate_limit.md
impact: You need to carefully tune in limits as per your environment.

Therefore, an automated approach is both feasible and efficient.

Its behavior was equivalent to turning off all admission controllers. Impact: Only requests explicitly allowed by the admissions control plugins would be served.

Default Value: AlwaysAdmit is not in the list of default admission plugins. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#alwaysadmit
impact: Only requests explicitly allowed by the admissions control plugins would be served.
