title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None
audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
remediation: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
impact: None
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
justification: Both AI opinions unanimously agree that the audit approach should be manual due to the inability of Kubernetes APIs and kubectl to directly expose the CNI plugin type or its capabilities. The verification process requires a thorough review of configuration files and documentation, which cannot be automated. Therefore, a manual approach is necessary to ensure compliance with the control.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the CNI plugin in use: - Check the kubelet or kube-controller-manager startup arguments for the `--network-plugin` flag. - Review the CNI manifest deployed in the `kube-system` namespace by running `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` and look for pods named after common CNIs such as calico, weave, flannel, cilium, etc. 2. Determine the version of the CNI plugin installed, if possible, by checking pod image tags or deployment manifests. 3. Consult the official documentation for the identified CNI plugin and version to verify support for both Ingress and Egress network policies. 4. Confirm that the documentation explicitly states support for Kubernetes NetworkPolicy objects, including both Ingress and Egress rules. 5. Mark as compliant if the CNI plugin in use supports both Ingress and Egress network policies; otherwise, mark as non-compliant.

title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace. Impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces``` Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them. Default Value: By default, network policies are not created. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/networkpolicies/ 2. https://octetz.com/posts/k8s-network-policy-apis 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/declare-network-policy/
impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
justification: The task of ensuring that all namespaces have Network Policies defined can be efficiently automated using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl` commands, as both AI opinions confirm. The proposed automation details are technically accurate and feasible, allowing for a systematic check of each namespace's compliance with the control requirements.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: NetworkPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces` and `kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace>` - Properties to Check: Each namespace must have at least one NetworkPolicy object. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace in the cluster has at least one NetworkPolicy defined. - Fail Criteria: Any namespace exists without at least one NetworkPolicy.

title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
rationale: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs. Impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
audit: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
remediation: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables. Default Value: By default, secrets are not defined References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
justification: The control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes CLI commands, as both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation. The first opinion provides a comprehensive automated approach using `kubectl`, and the second opinion supports this classification while suggesting improvements. Given the nature of the control, which involves checking configurations that are accessible through Kubernetes APIs, an automated solution is both practical and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Deployments, ReplicaSets, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs - API/CLI Method: kubectl - Properties to Check: Inspect the `spec.template.spec.containers` for `env` and `envFrom` fields to identify any references to secrets via `secretKeyRef` or `secretRef`. - Pass Criteria: No containers in any workload resource reference secrets via `env` or `envFrom` (i.e., all secrets are mounted as files/volumes instead). - Fail Criteria: Any container in any workload resource references a secret via `env` or `envFrom`.

title: Consider external secret storage
description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
rationale: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments. Impact: None
audit: Review your secrets management implementation.
remediation: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution. Default Value: By default, no external secret management is configured.
impact: None
justification: The control requires a comprehensive review of the architecture and implementation of secret management, which involves subjective assessments that cannot be fully automated through Kubernetes APIs or CLI commands. While some aspects can be partially automated, the overall determination of compliance hinges on evaluating external secret management solutions and their configurations, which necessitates manual inspection of documentation, deployment manifests, and possibly interviews with engineers.
manual_summary: 1. Review your Kubernetes cluster documentation and deployment manifests for references to external secrets management solutions (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, Google Secret Manager, or third-party controllers like External Secrets Operator). 2. Inspect how secrets are provisioned to workloads: - Execute `kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces` to identify any native Kubernetes Secret objects. - Run `kubectl get crds` to list CustomResourceDefinitions and check for custom resources (e.g., `ExternalSecret`, `SecretProviderClass`) that indicate integration with external secret stores. 3. Validate that the external secrets solution requires authentication to access secrets, provides audit logging, and encrypts secrets at rest and in transit by reviewing the configuration and documentation of the external secrets management solution.

title: Configure Image Provenance using ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller
description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
rationale: Kubernetes supports plugging in provenance rules to accept or reject the images in your deployments. You could configure such rules to ensure that only approved images are deployed in the cluster. Impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that image provenance is configured as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup image provenance. Default Value: By default, image provenance is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#imagepolicywebhook 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design- proposals/image-provenance.md 3. https://hub.docker.com/r/dnurmi/anchore-toolbox/ 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/22888
impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
justification: The control can be effectively audited using automated methods by querying the Kubernetes API server configuration to verify the presence of the `ImagePolicyWebhook` admission controller. Both AI opinions support the automated approach, and the use of `kubectl` commands allows for a streamlined verification process. The automation can be enhanced by leveraging Kubernetes SDKs, but the current method is sufficient for compliance checks.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: API Server - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` - Properties to Check: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag in the API server configuration - Pass Criteria: `ImagePolicyWebhook` is present in the `--enable-admission-plugins` list, and the referenced webhook configuration file exists and is properly configured. - Fail Criteria: `ImagePolicyWebhook` is absent from the enabled admission plugins, or the webhook configuration file is missing or not referenced.

title: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
rationale: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default. You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users. Impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
audit: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them. Default Value: By default, Kubernetes starts with 4 initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with- objects/namespaces/#viewing-namespaces 2. http://blog.kubernetes.io/2016/08/security-best-practices-kubernetes- deployment.html 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-node/589- efficient-node-heartbeats
impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The control can be effectively assessed by enumerating namespaces and checking for the presence of non-default namespaces, which aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes. The automation details provided are technically accurate and feasible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces - API/CLI Method: kubectl get namespaces - Properties to Check: Existence of non-default namespaces - Pass Criteria: At least one non-default namespace exists (i.e., a namespace other than default, kube-system, kube-node-lease, kube-public). - Fail Criteria: Only the default namespaces exist, or all user-created resources are in the "default" namespace.

title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container. Impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault Default Value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ 2. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/
impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
justification: The task of ensuring that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in pod definitions can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. Both AI opinions support the automated approach, and the technical details provided are sufficient to implement a robust solution. The ability to programmatically check the seccomp profile settings across all pods and containers aligns with Kubernetes capabilities, making automation the most efficient and reliable method.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o yaml - Properties to Check: .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type - Pass Criteria: All Pods (and containers, if overridden) have seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault. - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container missing seccompProfile or set to Unconfined/Localhost.

title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context. Impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers. Default Value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/security-context/ 2. https://learn.cisecurity.org/benchmarks
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
justification: Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach for checking the presence of security contexts in Pods and containers. The task can be efficiently performed using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools like `kubectl`, which allows for straightforward data retrieval and validation without the need for human judgment. The automation details provided are technically sound and feasible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json - Properties to Check: pod.spec.securityContext and container.securityContext - Pass Criteria: Each Pod must have either a pod-level securityContext or each container within the Pod must have a container-level securityContext defined. - Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container that lacks a securityContext field (i.e., the field is missing or empty) is non-compliant.

title: The default namespace should not be used
description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
rationale: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources. Impact: None
audit: Run this command to list objects in default namespace kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default The only entries there should be system managed resources such as the kubernetes service
remediation: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace. Default Value: Unless a namespace is specific on object creation, the default namespace will be used
impact: None
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands, making it suitable for automation. The provided command accurately lists all namespaced resources in the "default" namespace, and the criteria for compliance are clear and actionable. Given the technical capabilities of Kubernetes, an automated approach is not only feasible but also efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaced resources - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default` - Properties to Check: Existence of user-created resources in the "default" namespace - Pass Criteria: Only system-managed resources (e.g., the "kubernetes" service) exist in the "default" namespace - Fail Criteria: Any user-created resource (other than the default "kubernetes" service) exists in the "default" namespace. This program will help ensure compliance with the control by identifying any non-compliant resources in the default namespace.
